The Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector lets you adjust the trim angle of the watercraft while riding. It is located on the left handlebar grip and works by changing the jet thrust nozzle angle vertically, which raises or lowers the bow attitude of the jetski.

<PIC>

To operate the QSTS selector, use the selector together with the shift lock lever:

1. Reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less.
2. Squeeze the shift lock lever.
3. Turn the QSTS selector to the desired trim position.
4. Release the shift lock lever to lock the selector in place.

Important: Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating at full throttle, as this could damage the QSTS mechanism.

The QSTS has 5 trim positions:

- 2 bow-down positions
- Neutral “N”
- 2 bow-up positions

<PIC>

The neutral “N” position is suitable for most normal operating conditions and generally provides balanced performance. You can choose bow-down or bow-up trim when you want to change the watercraft’s handling or performance characteristics.

Bow-up trim raises the front of the watercraft when it is on plane. This puts less of the bow in the water, reducing water resistance. As a result, straight-line acceleration while on plane and top speed may improve. However, in some conditions the watercraft may begin to “porpoise,” meaning it hops or bounces on the water. If this happens, return the trim to neutral or select a bow-down position.

<PIC>

In short: use neutral for general riding, bow-up for improved on-plane acceleration and top speed when conditions allow, and switch back to neutral or bow-down if the ride becomes unstable or starts porpoising.,['Manual20_51', 'Manual20_52', 'Manual20_53']

On your 2005 WaveRunner, the fuel meter and hour meter are both part of the multifunction information center. They help you monitor fuel level while riding and track engine operating time for maintenance planning. The display layout includes the fuel meter on the right side of the gauge and the hour meter in the center digital display area. When the engine starts, the display segments light up briefly and the buzzer sounds twice, then the meter returns to normal operation after a few seconds. Fuel meter The fuel meter is used to check the approximate amount of fuel remaining in the tank while you are riding. It has 8 segments, and more lit segments mean more fuel remaining. Key points: - The 8 segments show the fuel level in steps, not as an exact measurement. - The reading can change depending on riding conditions, watercraft angle, acceleration, and fuel movement in the tank. - Use it as a reference, not as an exact fuel quantity gauge. - When fuel drops to about 13 L / 3.4 US gal / 2.9 Imp gal, the lowest two fuel segments will blink as a low-fuel warning. The fuel warning indicator and warning light may also blink, and the buzzer may sound intermittently. - After refueling, the warning clears when the engine is restarted. Hour meter The hour meter shows the total amount of time the engine has operated since the watercraft was new. It is mainly used to help you keep track of service and maintenance intervals. Key points: - It records actual engine running hours, not calendar time. - The reading is retained even if the battery terminals are disconnected. - If the hour meter malfunctions, the display may show “_-_” and the warning light will blink. In short: the fuel meter helps you estimate how much fuel is left while riding, and the hour meter helps you track total engine use for maintenance.,['Manual20_54', 'Manual20_58', 'Manual20_59']

Yes—inspecting the fuel filter and fuel tank is an important part of keeping your WaveRunner’s fuel system safe and reliable. Here’s how to check them and what to do if you find a problem.

## 1. Fuel filter inspection

Your watercraft is equipped with a one-piece, disposable fuel filter. During inspection, locate the fuel filter and check it for signs of water or contamination.

<PIC>

### What to check
- Look for water inside the fuel filter.
- Check for dirt, debris, or discoloration that may indicate fuel contamination.
- Inspect the surrounding area for any signs of gasoline leakage or fuel smell.

### Replacement interval
The fuel filter should be replaced:
- After the initial 10 hours of operation or the first month, whichever comes first.
- Every 200 hours or 24 months thereafter.
- Any time water is found in the filter.

### Important safety warning
Do not try to replace the fuel filter yourself. If the filter is installed incorrectly, gasoline can leak, which may create a fire or explosion hazard. If the fuel filter needs replacement, have a Yamaha dealer replace it.

## 2. Fuel tank inspection

Check the fuel tank for leakage and for any signs of water in the tank or fuel system.

<PIC>

### What to check
- Inspect the fuel tank area for gasoline leakage.
- Smell for strong fuel odor, which may indicate a leak.
- Look for signs of water contamination in the fuel system.
- Check whether the tank appears dirty or needs cleaning.

### If water or contamination is found
If water is found in the fuel system, or if the fuel tank needs to be cleaned, do not continue operating the watercraft until it has been serviced. Have a Yamaha dealer inspect and service the fuel system.

## Quick safety tips
- Work in a well-ventilated area away from flames, sparks, or smoking materials.
- If you smell gasoline or see fuel leakage, stop inspection and avoid starting the engine.
- For fuel filter replacement or fuel tank cleaning, use dealer service rather than attempting the repair yourself.,['Manual20_81', 'Manual20_82']

Yes. The adjustable sponsons can be repositioned to fine-tune your WaveRunner’s handling based on your riding preference and water conditions.

<PIC>

There are two main adjustment directions:

- Up position: Reduces water resistance. This can improve straight-line acceleration when the craft is on plane and may help increase top speed.
- Down position: Gives the watercraft more “hook” in the water. This improves turning grip and enhances cornering performance.

<PIC>

To adjust the sponsons:

1. Remove the mounting bolts on both sponsons.

<PIC>

2. Remove both sponsons, then reinstall them in the desired position.

Important: Make sure both left and right sponsons are installed at the same level. If they are uneven, handling may become unbalanced.

3. Tighten the bolts on both sponsons securely to the specified torque.

<PIC>

Tightening torque: 18 N·m / 1.8 kgf·m / 13 ft·lb

After adjustment, test the watercraft carefully in open water at moderate speed first so you can confirm the handling feel before riding aggressively.,['Manual20_69', 'Manual20_70', 'Manual20_83', 'Manual20_84']

If weeds, grass, or other debris are caught in the jet intake or impeller, the jet pump may cavitate. A common sign is that the engine speed rises, but the watercraft has reduced thrust or poor acceleration. If you continue operating in this condition, the engine can overheat and may be seriously damaged.

<PIC>

Before cleaning, follow these safety steps carefully:

1. Beach or secure the watercraft in a safe place.
2. Shut the engine off completely.
3. Remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch so the engine cannot accidentally start.

WARNING: Never put your hands, tools, clothing, or any object near the jet intake, impeller, or jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running. Contact with rotating jet pump parts can cause severe injury or death.

To clean the jet intake and impeller:

1. Turn the watercraft onto its port, or left, side as shown below. Place a clean cloth, mat, or carpeting underneath the hull first to help prevent scratches and abrasion. Support the bow while turning it over so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.

<PIC>

2. Check and remove any weeds, rope, plastic, or other debris from the following areas:
   - Jet intake / intake grate
   - Drive shaft area
   - Impeller
   - Pump housing
   - Jet thrust nozzle

3. After the debris is removed, visually confirm that the intake and nozzle area are clear before restarting the engine.

If the debris is tightly wrapped around the drive shaft or impeller, or you cannot remove it easily, do not force it with sharp tools. Please have a Yamaha dealer inspect and clean it to avoid damaging the pump components.

To help prevent this from happening again, avoid operating in areas with heavy weed growth whenever possible. If you must ride through weedy areas, vary the throttle between partial and full throttle rather than running steadily at trolling speed, since weeds tend to collect more easily at a constant low speed.,['Manual20_85', 'Manual20_86']

Here’s how to start your jetski safely in different situations.

### 1. Basic engine starting procedure
Before starting the engine, always make sure the engine shut-off cord is attached to your wrist, and the clip is correctly installed on the engine shut-off switch. This is an important safety step: if you fall off, the clip will pull out and stop the engine automatically.

<PIC>

After the clip is installed, press the green start switch to start the engine.

<PIC>

Please note: the engine will not start if the clip is removed from the engine shut-off switch.

---

### 2. Starting and boarding in shallow water
If you are starting from shallow water, do not start the engine unless the water is at least 60 cm / 2 ft deep. Starting in water that is too shallow can pull sand, stones, weeds, or debris into the jet intake and may damage the propulsion system.

<PIC>

Steps:
1. Move the watercraft into water that is at least 60 cm / 2 ft deep.
2. Board the watercraft from the side or rear.
3. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist, then install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
4. Hold the handlebars with both hands.
5. Place both feet flat in the footwells.
6. Start the engine and move off carefully.

---

### 3. Starting from a dock
When starting from a dock, board carefully and make sure the watercraft is clear of the dock before accelerating.

Steps:
1. Board the watercraft from the side.
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist and install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
3. Push the watercraft away from the dock so it has enough clearance.
4. Hold the handlebars with both hands and place both feet in the footwells.
5. Start the engine, check your surroundings, and then proceed slowly.

<PIC>

---

### 4. Boarding and starting when riding alone
If you are in the water and need to board alone, always board from the rear to keep the watercraft balanced.

Steps:
1. Swim to the rear of the watercraft. Place both hands on the boarding platform, pull yourself up, and grasp the handgrip with one hand.

<PIC>

2. Pull yourself up to a kneeling position on the platform, then move forward onto the seat and sit astride.

<PIC>

3. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your left wrist and install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
4. Hold the handlebars firmly with both hands.
5. Place both feet in the footwells.
6. Look in all directions to confirm the area is clear.
7. Start the engine and move off smoothly.

<PIC>

---

### 5. Boarding and starting with a passenger
When carrying a passenger, stability and timing are very important. Do not apply throttle until the passenger is fully seated, has both feet in the footwells, and is holding on securely.

Steps:
1. The operator boards first and sits astride the seat.
2. Attach the engine shut-off cord to the operator’s wrist and install the clip on the engine shut-off switch.
3. Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.
4. The passenger should board from the rear and sit behind the operator. Both operator and passenger should help keep the watercraft balanced during this step.

<PIC>

5. Confirm the passenger has both feet in the footwells and is holding on securely to the operator.
6. Start the engine.
7. Look carefully in all directions to make sure there are no boats, swimmers, obstacles, or other hazards nearby.
8. Accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

---

### Important safety reminders
- Always scan the area before starting off.
- Keep a safe distance from swimmers, boats, docks, and obstacles.
- Do not apply throttle while a passenger is still boarding or not seated securely.
- Remember that the watercraft does not have brakes.
- You need throttle to steer effectively, so avoid releasing the throttle completely when trying to turn away from an object.
- Remove the engine shut-off clip when the engine is not running to prevent accidental starting or unauthorized use.,['Manual20_44', 'Manual20_48', 'Manual20_88', 'Manual20_89', 'Manual20_71', 'Manual20_72', 'Manual20_73', 'Manual20_90', 'Manual20_92']

Here are the basic steps for boarding your jetski with one passenger in deep water:

1. **Operator boards first.** Climb onto the watercraft first and sit astride the seat. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, then install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.

2. **Passenger moves to the rear.** Have the passenger swim/move to the rear of the watercraft and prepare to board from the back.

3. **Passenger boards and sits down.** The passenger should climb onto the rear platform, move carefully onto the seat, and sit behind the operator.

<PIC>

**Tip:** While the passenger is boarding, both the operator and passenger should work together to keep the watercraft balanced and stable.

4. **Confirm passenger position before starting.** Make sure the passenger has both feet placed on the floor of the footwell and is securely holding on to the operator.

5. **Start and move off safely.** Start the engine, look carefully in all directions for boats, swimmers, and obstacles, then accelerate smoothly to planing speed.

<PIC>

<PIC>

**Important safety reminder:** Do not apply throttle until the passenger is fully seated, has both feet in the footwell, and is holding on to the operator. Before starting off, always confirm the area around you is clear of boats, obstructions, and swimmers.

If the watercraft is difficult to balance while stopped in deep water, use this alternative method:

1. **Passenger steadies the watercraft while the operator boards.** The passenger should hold the watercraft steady from the water. The operator then pulls onto the rear platform, moves into a seated position, and balances the watercraft.

<PIC>

2. **Operator prepares the engine.** Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, install the clip to the engine shut-off switch, start the engine, and keep it at idle.

3. **Passenger boards as the craft begins moving.** The passenger pulls onto the rear platform, kneels, and then crawls onto the seat as the watercraft begins to accelerate.

<PIC>

4. **Accelerate smoothly.** Once both riders are properly seated and secure, accelerate to planing speed, then reduce the throttle to your desired running speed.

<PIC>

Also, do not operate the watercraft if the combined weight of the operator, passenger, and any cargo exceeds **160 kg (353 lb)**.,['Manual20_90', 'Manual20_91', 'Manual20_92', 'jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

If the jetski is hard to balance while stopped, use the following boarding method. This is especially helpful when riding with a passenger, because the craft is less stable at a standstill.

1. Have the passenger steady the jetski first. The passenger should remain in the water and hold the jetski steady while the operator boards from the rear. The operator should pull themself up onto the boarding platform, move into a seated position, and then balance the jetski.

<PIC>

2. Once the operator is seated and balanced, attach the lanyard to your left wrist, then install the lanyard clip onto the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle speed.

3. Next, the passenger should board from the rear. The passenger pulls themself onto the platform, gets into a kneeling position, and then crawls forward onto the seat as the jetski begins to accelerate smoothly.

<PIC>

4. After the passenger is seated securely with both feet in the footwell and holding on to the operator, accelerate the jetski to planing speed. Once the craft becomes stable on plane, the operator can reduce the throttle to the desired running speed.

<PIC>

Important: The heavier the combined weight of the operator, passenger, and any cargo, the harder the jetski will be to balance. Do not operate the jetski if the total load exceeds 160 kg (353 lb), including cargo.,['jetski_06', 'jetski_07', 'jetski_08']

The maximum load of this jetski is 160 kg (353 lb). This maximum load includes the combined weight of the operator, one passenger, and any cargo. The watercraft is rated for a maximum of 2 people total—the operator plus one passenger—so do not carry more than two riders at the same time. For safe operation, make sure the total weight on board stays within the 160 kg / 353 lb limit, including any items stored on the watercraft.,['Manual20_12']

Here is an overview of the base station for the XL490 and XL495 landline models: For XL490 The XL490 base station has a simple layout with one main button on the top of the base: • Find handset / paging button: Press this button to make the registered handset ring, which helps you locate it if it is nearby. • Registration mode: Press and hold this button to put the base station into registration mode when adding or pairing a handset. For XL495 The XL495 base station includes the same handset location and registration function, plus additional controls for the answering machine: • Find handset / paging button: Press to find registered handsets. • Registration mode: Press and hold to enter registration mode for pairing handsets. • Speaker: Plays answering machine messages and related audio from the base station. • Answering machine on/off button: Turns the answering machine function on or off. • Delete button: Deletes the current message during playback; press and hold to delete old messages. • Volume down/up buttons: Adjust the speaker volume on the base station. • Skip backward/forward buttons: Move to the previous or next message during playback. • Play/stop button: Starts or stops message playback. In short, the XL490 base station mainly provides handset paging and registration functions, while the XL495 base station adds built-in answering machine controls directly on the base.,['Manual22_18', 'Manual22_19']

To connect the base station of your landline phone, please follow these steps:

1. Check the power adapter first
Before connecting, make sure the voltage of the power adapter matches the voltage shown on the back or underside of the phone. To avoid damage, use only the supplied power adapter.

2. Connect the power adapter
Plug one end of the power adapter into the DC input jack on the bottom of the base station, then plug the other end into a wall power socket.

<PIC>

3. Connect the telephone line cord
Plug one end of the telephone line cord into the telephone socket on the bottom of the base station, then plug the other end into the telephone wall socket.

Note: In some countries, you may need to connect a line adapter to the line cord first, and then plug it into the telephone wall socket.

4. If you use DSL internet service
If your telephone line also carries DSL high-speed internet service, install a DSL filter between the telephone line cord and the telephone wall socket. This helps prevent line noise and caller ID problems caused by DSL interference.

After these connections are complete, place the handset on the base station to charge before first use.,['Manual22_22']

To install the handset, please follow these steps:

1. **Check the safety precautions first**  
Before connecting or installing the handset, make sure the phone and accessories are used only with the supplied/compatible power adapter, batteries, and cables. Avoid letting metal objects touch the charging contacts or battery contacts.

<PIC>

2. **Remove the battery pull tab**  
The batteries are already installed inside the handset. Before charging, pull the battery tape straight out from the battery door area as shown below. This allows the batteries to connect properly.

<PIC>

**Important:** If you ever need to reinsert or replace the batteries, check the battery polarity carefully. Incorrect polarity may damage the handset.

3. **Charge the handset on the base station**  
Place the handset onto the base station. When it is positioned correctly, you should hear a docking sound, and the handset will begin charging.

4. **Charge before first use**  
For best performance, charge the handset for **8 hours before using it for the first time**. It is normal if the handset becomes slightly warm while charging.,['Manual22_20', 'Manual22_23']

You can check the battery level directly on the handset display. Look for the battery icon at the top of the screen—the bars inside the icon show the current battery level.

<PIC>

Here is how to read the battery icon:

- Full battery: the icon shows three bars.
- Medium battery: the icon shows two bars.
- Low battery: the icon shows one bar.
- Very low battery: an empty battery icon flashes, which means the handset needs to be recharged.

When the handset is placed correctly on the base station/charger, the bars in the battery icon scroll or animate to show that charging is in progress.

<PIC>

Please note: if the batteries become completely empty, the handset will turn off. If you are on a call when the battery is almost empty, you will hear warning tones first, and the call may disconnect shortly after the warning. To avoid this, place the handset back on the base station to recharge when the battery icon is low or flashing.,['Manual22_28', 'Manual22_29']

If your landline handset stays in “Searching” status, it usually means the handset is not properly connected to the base station. Please try the following steps:

1. Check that the base station has power
Make sure the power adapter is firmly connected to the base station and plugged into a working wall socket. If the base station has no power, the handset will not be able to find it.

<PIC>

2. Register the handset to the base station
If the handset is not paired with the base station, register it again. On the base station, press and hold the paging/register button to enter registration mode, then follow the handset prompts to complete registration.

<PIC>

3. Move the handset closer to the base station
The handset may be out of range. Place it near the base station and wait a moment to see whether the signal bars return.

If the handset still remains in “Searching” status after these checks, restart both the handset and base station, then try the registration process again.,['Manual22_22', 'Manual22_18']

You can identify the phone’s current status by checking the LED indicator on both the handset and the base station. The meaning depends on whether the LED is steady on, off, flashing, or flashing quickly. 1. Handset LED indicator You can choose whether the handset LED shows event status or charging status. To set it: Select [Menu] > [Phone setup] > [LED status] > [Events status] or [Charge status], then press [OK] to confirm. When set to Events status:
  • LED on: Audio boost is on.
  • LED off: The phone is in a call or in standby mode.
  • LED flashing: This may indicate one of the following:
    • There is a new message.
    • There is a new voicemail.
    • There is a missed call.
    • The phone is muted.
When set to Charge status:
  • LED on:
    • The handset is placed on the base or charging cradle in standby mode.
    • Audio boost is on during a call.
  • LED off: The handset is not on the base or charging cradle.
  • LED flashing: The phone is muted.
2. Base station LED indicator The base station LED helps you quickly check message, call, paging, playback, and registration status. Base station LED behavior:
  • Steady on:
    • There is no new message and the memory is not full.
    • The handset is switched on.
    • The phone has entered paging mode.
  • Flashing:
    • There is a new message and the memory is not full.
    • There is an incoming call.
    • The phone is recording an incoming message or announcement.
    • Messages are being played back.
    • Remote access is active, or messages are being listened to from the handset.
  • Flashing fast:
    • There is no new message and the memory is full.
    • The base station has entered registration mode.
Note: The answering machine and base station message LED functions are available only for the XL495 model.,['Manual22_40', 'Manual22_46']

You can identify the current status of the landline base station by checking how the LED indicator behaves. The meanings are as follows:

<PIC>

**1. LED is steady on**
This usually means the base station is in a normal ready state. It may indicate:
- There is **no new message**, and the answering machine memory is **not full**.
- The handset is switched on.
- The base station has entered **paging mode** to help locate the handset.

**2. LED is flashing**
A flashing LED usually indicates an active event or message-related activity, such as:
- There is a **new message**, and the memory is **not full**.
- There is an **incoming call**.
- The phone is **recording an incoming message or announcement**.
- Messages are being played back.
- Remote access is in use, or messages are being listened to from the handset.

**3. LED is flashing fast**
A fast flashing LED indicates a special status that may need attention:
- There is **no new message**, but the memory is **full**.
- The base station has entered **registration mode**, usually for registering an additional handset.

So, in short: **steady on = normal/ready status**, **flashing = call or message activity**, and **fast flashing = memory full or registration mode**.,['Manual22_46']

To lower the roll bar on the lawn mower, follow these steps carefully:

1. Park the mower on a level surface, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.

2. Remove the hairpin cotters, then pull out the 2 pins that secure the roll bar.

3. Lower the roll bar into the down position. There are 2 available down positions, as shown below—choose the position that provides the clearance you need.

<PIC>

4. Reinstall the 2 pins through the roll bar mounting holes, then secure each pin with its hairpin cotter. Make sure both pins and cotters are fully seated so the roll bar is locked in place.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:

- Lower the roll bar only when absolutely necessary, such as when passing under a low obstruction.
- There is no rollover protection when the roll bar is in the down position.
- Do not wear the seat belt when the roll bar is down.
- Drive slowly and carefully while the roll bar is lowered.
- Raise and lock the roll bar again as soon as overhead clearance permits.
- Before driving under branches, doorways, electrical wires, or other overhead objects, check clearance carefully and avoid contact.,['Manual23_32', 'Manual23_33']

For machines equipped with the Suspension System only, you can adjust the 2 rear-shock assemblies to change the ride feel and improve comfort. Move both shock assemblies to a position that feels best for your weight, terrain, and preferred ride stiffness.

<PIC>

Follow these steps:

1. Park the mower on a level surface, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.
2. On one rear-shock assembly, flip the quick-release pin lever down to the unlocked position.
3. Slide the top of the shock assembly left or right along the slot to adjust the suspension feel:
   - Move toward the softer setting for a smoother, more cushioned ride.
   - Move toward the firmer setting for a stiffer ride and more support.
4. Adjust the opposite rear-shock assembly to the same position. The left and right shock assemblies must always be matched symmetrically.
5. Flip each quick-release pin lever up to the locked position to secure the shock assemblies.
6. Confirm that both sides are fully locked before operating the mower.

Note: The slots include detent positions as reference points, but the shock assemblies do not have to be placed only in those detents. You may position them anywhere in the slot, as long as both rear shocks are set to the same position.,['Manual23_38']

To adjust the height of cut on a mower equipped with an electric deck lift, follow these steps:

1. Raise the mower deck by pushing up on the deck-lift switch. The switch is located on the motion-control lever; pushing it upward raises the deck, while pushing it downward lowers the deck.

<PIC>

2. Choose the desired cutting height by selecting the matching hole in the height-of-cut bracket. Insert the height-of-cut pin fully into that hole to lock in the selected height setting.

<PIC>

3. Lower the mower deck by pushing down on the deck-lift switch. The deck will lower until it rests at the height set by the pin.

Important: Whenever you change the height of cut, also adjust the anti-scalp rollers to match the new cutting height. This helps prevent the deck from scalping the turf, especially on uneven ground.,['Manual23_55', 'Manual23_56']

To load the lawn mower onto a trailer or truck safely, please follow these steps:

1. Use the right transport equipment
   - Use a heavy-duty trailer or truck suitable for the machine’s weight.
   - Use a full-width ramp only—do not use separate narrow ramps for each side.
   - Make sure the ramp angle is safe: the angle between the ramp and the ground must not exceed 15°.
   - The ramp should be at least 4 times as long as the height from the ground to the trailer or truck bed.

<PIC>

2. Prepare the trailer or truck
   - If using a trailer, connect it securely to the towing vehicle.
   - Attach the safety chains.
   - If equipped, connect the trailer brakes and lights.
   - Lower the ramp and confirm it is stable, centered, and within the 15° maximum angle.

3. Load the mower correctly
   - Use extreme caution while operating on the ramp.
   - Back the machine up the ramp onto the trailer or truck.
   - When unloading later, drive the machine forward down the ramp.
   - Avoid sudden acceleration, sudden braking, or sharp steering while on the ramp, as this can cause loss of control or a tip-over.

<PIC>

4. Secure the mower after loading
   - Once the mower is positioned on the trailer or truck, shut off the engine.
   - Remove the key.
   - Engage the parking brake.
   - Tie down the machine using straps, chains, cables, or ropes.
   - Secure it near the front caster wheels and near the rear bumper using the tie-down points.
   - Follow local requirements for tie-down methods and transport regulations.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Use only a full-width ramp.
- Do not exceed a 15° ramp angle.
- Make sure the ramp is long enough—at least 4 times the trailer or truck bed height from the ground.
- Back the mower up the ramp when loading.
- Drive the mower forward down the ramp when unloading.
- Move slowly and smoothly; avoid sudden acceleration or deceleration.
- Do not drive the mower on public streets or roadways.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64', 'Manual23_65']

To unload the lawn mower safely from a trailer or truck, follow these steps:

1. Lower the full-width ramp and check the ramp angle. Make sure the angle between the ramp and the ground does not exceed 15°. The ramp should also be long enough—at least 4 times the height of the trailer or truck bed from the ground—to help keep the angle safe and stable.

<PIC>

2. Drive the machine forward down the ramp slowly and in a straight line. Do not back the machine down the ramp.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
• Use only a full-width ramp. Do not use separate narrow ramps for each side of the machine.
• Use extreme caution while driving on the ramp.
• Avoid sudden acceleration, braking, or sharp steering movements, as these can cause loss of control or a tip-over.
• Keep bystanders away from the unloading area until the machine is completely off the ramp.,['Manual23_63', 'Manual23_64']

Here’s how to change the engine oil on the lawn mower. The procedure is very similar for Kawasaki and Kohler engines; use the correct drain/fill steps for your engine type.

1. Warm the oil
Start the engine and let it run for about 5 minutes. Warm oil flows more easily and drains more completely.

2. Position the machine for draining
Park the mower so the oil-drain side is slightly lower than the opposite side. This helps the old oil drain out fully.

3. Secure the mower
Disengage the blade-control switch/PTO, engage the parking brake, shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operator position.

4. Drain the old engine oil
Place a suitable drain pan under the drain hose. Pull the drain hose out, route it into the pan, loosen and remove the drain plug, and allow the oil to drain completely. After draining, reinstall and securely tighten the drain plug.

For Kawasaki engines, follow the drain-hose sequence shown below:
<PIC>

For Kohler engines, the oil-draining process is shown here:
<PIC>

5. Add new engine oil gradually
Pour in about 80% of the specified engine-oil amount first. Wait a few minutes for the oil to settle, then add small amounts as needed until the level reaches the FULL mark. Do not overfill.

For Kawasaki engines, add and check the oil level as shown below. When checking the level, insert the dipstick as shown and confirm the oil reaches the FULL mark before tightening the cap.
<PIC>

For Kohler engines, use the same gradual-fill approach shown here: add oil, wait about 3 minutes, check the dipstick, and tighten the cap once the oil is at the FULL mark.
<PIC>

6. Recheck after running
Start the engine, move the mower to a flat, level area, shut it off, and check the oil level again. Add oil only if needed to bring it back to the FULL mark.

Important notes:
- Dispose of used oil properly at a recycling center.
- Change the engine oil every 100 hours, or more often in dirty or dusty conditions.
- Running the engine with too little or too much oil can damage the engine.
- If your mower has an oil filter and you are changing it at the same time, refill according to the correct oil capacity for your engine model.,['Manual23_75', 'Manual23_86', 'Manual23_76', 'Manual23_87']

You can remove the lawn mower’s air-cleaner filters by following the steps below. The procedure depends on whether your machine has a Kawasaki engine or a Kohler engine, so first identify which engine is installed, then follow the matching section.

Before starting, always make the machine safe:
1. Park the machine on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch / PTO.
3. Engage the parking brake.
4. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop.

For Kawasaki engines:
1. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
2. Pull the air-cleaner cover away from the air-cleaner body.

<PIC>

3. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
4. Gently slide the primary filter straight out of the air-cleaner body. Try not to bump or knock the filter against the side of the housing, as this can drop dirt into the intake area.
5. Remove the safety filter only if you are replacing it. Do not remove it just for cleaning.

For Kohler engines:
1. Release the latches on the air cleaner.
2. Pull the air-inlet cover off the air-cleaner body.
3. Clean the air-inlet screen and the cover.
4. Reinstall the air-inlet cover and secure it with the latches.
5. Release the latches on the air cleaner again, then pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.

<PIC>

6. Clean the inside of the air-cleaner cover with compressed air.
7. Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body. Avoid striking the filter against the side of the body.
8. Remove the inner filter only if you are going to replace it.

Important filter care notes:
- Do not attempt to clean the safety filter or inner filter. If it is dirty, replace it.
- If the safety/inner filter is dirty, replace both the safety/inner filter and the primary filter.
- Do not clean the primary filter. Replace it if it is dirty, bent, damaged, or shows any signs of deterioration.
- Do not operate the engine unless both filters and the cover are correctly installed, as this helps prevent engine damage from dust and debris.,['Manual23_72', 'Manual23_83']

To replace the mower belt, follow the steps below. For safety, make sure the mower is fully shut down before working near the deck, pulleys, or belt.

1. Park the machine on a level surface.
2. Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO) and engage the parking brake.
3. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait until all moving parts have completely stopped.
4. Lower the mower deck to the 76 mm / 3 inch height-of-cut position.
5. Remove the belt covers from the mower deck. Press the retaining tab down, slide the cover out, and remove the cover from the deck as shown below.

<PIC>

6. Insert a 3/8-inch ratchet into the square hole in the idler arm.
7. Use the ratchet to relieve tension from the spring-loaded idler assembly.
8. With the belt tension released, remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley under the engine.
9. Remove the belt guide from the spring-loaded idler arm.
10. Remove the old mower belt.
11. Route the new belt around the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley under the engine. Make sure the belt follows the pulley path shown below and sits properly in each pulley groove.

<PIC>

In the belt-routing image:
1. Spring  
2. Clutch pulley  
3. Mower belt  
4. Spring-loaded idler assembly  
5. Ratchet  
6. Square hole in the idler arm for the ratchet  

12. Reinstall the belt guide on the idler arm.
13. Using the 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole, reinstall the idler spring.
14. Check that both ends of the spring are fully seated in their anchor grooves.
15. Reinstall the belt covers. Slide each cover into position, press it down, and confirm that the tab locks securely into the slot on the deck.

<PIC>

After installation, visually check the belt routing again before operating the mower. The belt should be replaced if it shows signs of wear, such as squealing while rotating, blades slipping while cutting, frayed edges, burn marks, or cracks.,['Manual23_99', 'Manual23_100', 'Manual23_101']

To adjust the control settings on your over-the-range microwave, use the Control Set-Up pad on the control panel. This feature lets you change the microwave’s default settings, including the sound, clock display, display scroll speed, and defrost weight unit.

Control Set-Up options
After touching Control Set-Up, select the setting you want to change by pressing the matching number pad:

2. Clock ON/OFF control
  • Touch 1 for Clock ON
  • Touch 2 for Clock OFF

3. Scroll Speed control
  • Touch 1 for Slow speed

4. LBS/KG choice control

Follow these steps:

2. Touch number 4 to select LBS/KG choice.

The display will show KG, confirming that the defrost weight unit has been changed.,['Manual24_9', 'Manual24_10']

You can set the cooktop/countertop light timer on an over-the-range microwave so the **Lo Light** turns on and off automatically at the same times every day. Once set, it will continue repeating daily until you reset or cancel it.

For example, to set the light to **turn on at 2:00 AM** and **turn off at 7:00 AM**, follow these steps:

<PIC>

1. Touch the **Light Timer** pad.  
   - Press it **once for AM** or **twice for PM**.
2. Enter the time you want the light to turn on.  
   - For **2:00**, touch **[2] [0] [0]**.
3. Touch the **Light Timer** pad again.  
   - Press it **once for AM** or **twice for PM** to select the off-time period.
4. Enter the time you want the light to turn off.  
   - For **7:00**, touch **[7] [0] [0]**.
5. Touch **Start** to save the light timer setting.

After this, the light will come on at the low setting at the programmed start time and turn off at the programmed end time each day.

**To change or reset the on/off times:** repeat the same setup steps above with the new times.

**To cancel the Light Timer setting completely:**

<PIC>

1. Touch the **Light Timer** pad.
2. Touch **Stop/Clear**.

If the light timer is currently running and you simply want to turn the light off during operation, touch the **Light HI/LO/Off** pad.,['Manual24_11', 'Manual24_12']

Sure — on this over-the-range microwave, the Favorite Recipe feature lets you save one cooking setting in memory, then quickly recall it later. To set a favorite recipe, for example, 2 minutes of cooking: 1. Touch the Favorite Recipe pad. The display will show: “1:30 TOUCH START OR ENTER NEW TIME”. 2. Enter your desired cook time using the number pads. For 2 minutes, touch [2] [0] [0]. The display will show: “2:00 TOUCH START OR POWER”. 3. Touch Start. The microwave will begin cooking, and the display will show the time counting down. To use/recall the saved favorite recipe later: 1. Touch the Favorite Recipe pad. The saved time will appear on the display, for example: “2:00 TOUCH START OR POWER”. 2. Touch Start to begin cooking. When cooking is finished, the microwave will beep four times and END will appear on the display. Note: The power level defaults to High, but you can change the power level if needed before starting.,['Manual24_16', 'Manual24_17']

You can reheat food in your over-the-range microwave using the Sensor Reheat feature. This option automatically detects steam from the food and sets the reheating time for you, so you do not need to enter a cooking time or power level manually.

The Reheat feature includes 3 food categories. Select the number that matches the food you are reheating:

<PIC>

For example, to reheat a casserole:

<PIC>

1. Place the food in a microwave-safe container.
2. Cover it loosely with a microwave-safe lid, waxed paper, or vented plastic wrap.
3. Touch the Reheat pad.
4. Choose the food category:
   - Casserole: touch [1]
   - Dinner Plate: touch [2]
   - Soup/Sauce: touch [3]
5. The display will show SENSING while the microwave measures the humidity coming from the food.
6. Once sensing is complete, the microwave will automatically determine the remaining heating time.
7. When reheating is finished, you will hear four beeps and END will appear on the display.

Recommended amounts for Sensor Reheat are:

<PIC>

For best reheating results:
- Use only microwave-safe containers.
- Make sure the outside of the container and the inside of the microwave are dry before starting, as moisture can affect the sensor.
- Cover food loosely rather than sealing it tightly, so steam can escape properly.
- Do not open the door or press Stop/Clear while SENSING is displayed.
- After the sensing period is complete and the remaining time appears, you may open the door if needed to stir, turn, or rearrange the food, then press Start to continue.,['Manual24_29', 'Manual24_30', 'Manual24_31']

Your over-the-range microwave has an Auto Defrost function preset for three food types: Meat, Poultry, and Fish. This is the recommended way to defrost frozen raw foods by weight.

<PIC>

To use Auto Defrost:

1. Touch the Auto Defrost pad.
2. Select the food category:
   - Touch 1 for Meat
   - Touch 2 for Poultry
   - Touch 3 for Fish
3. Enter the food weight using the number pads. The weight should be entered in pounds and tenths of a pound, such as 1.2 for 1.2 lbs.
4. Touch Start to begin defrosting.

Example: To defrost 1.2 lbs. of ground beef, touch Auto Defrost, select 1 for Meat, enter 1 and 2 for 1.2 lbs., then touch Start.

<PIC>

During the defrost cycle, the display will count down the remaining defrost time. The microwave will beep during the cycle to remind you to open the door and turn, separate, or rearrange the food. If any portions have already thawed, remove them. Place the still-frozen portions back in the microwave, close the door, and touch Start again to continue.

Helpful tips:

- The available weight range is 0.1–6.0 lbs.; do not use Auto Defrost for food over 6.0 lbs.
