The grease filter on your over-the-range microwave should be removed and cleaned regularly—at least once a month, or more often if you cook frequently with grease or oil.

1. Remove the grease filter
Slide the filter to one side, then pull it downward and push it toward the opposite side. The filter should drop out from the bottom of the microwave.

<PIC>

2. Clean the filter
Soak the grease filter in hot water with a mild detergent, then rinse it thoroughly. After rinsing, shake off excess water and allow it to dry completely before reinstalling.

Do not use ammonia or harsh/corrosive cleaners, as they can damage the aluminum filter. Also note that dishwasher cleaning may cause the aluminum filter to darken, so hand washing is recommended if you want to maintain its appearance.

<PIC>

3. Reinstall the filter
Once the filter is dry, slide it into the side slot first. Then push it upward and toward the center of the microwave until it locks securely into place.

<PIC>

Important: Do not operate the vent hood without the grease filter installed, as this may increase the risk of personal injury or property damage.,['Manual24_47', 'Manual24_48', 'Manual24_49']

If your over-the-range microwave is set up for indoor/recirculating venting, the charcoal filter should be replaced every 6 to 12 months, or more often if you cook frequently or notice lingering odors. The charcoal filter cannot be washed or reused.

Please follow these steps:

1. Disconnect power first. Unplug the microwave, or turn off power at the main power supply/circuit breaker.

2. Remove the vent grill screws. Locate the two mounting screws in the middle of the top vent grill and remove them.

<PIC>

3. Remove the vent grill. Tip the grill forward, then lift it out and set it aside.

4. Take out the old charcoal filter. Pull the used filter out from the top opening of the microwave.

<PIC>

5. Install the new charcoal filter. Slide the new filter into place. Make sure it sits at the same angled position shown below so it rests correctly inside the opening.

<PIC>

6. Reinstall the vent grill. Slide the bottom of the grill back into position first, then push the top in until it snaps into place.

7. Secure the grill. Reinstall the two mounting screws you removed earlier.

8. Restore power. Turn the power back on at the main power supply or plug the microwave back in, then reset the clock if needed.,['Manual24_50', 'Manual24_51', 'Manual24_52']

To replace the oven light in an over-the-range microwave, please follow these steps carefully:

1. **Disconnect power first.** Unplug the microwave or turn off power at the main power supply to prevent electrical shock.

2. **Remove the vent cover screws.** Locate and remove the two middle mounting screws on the vent cover at the top front of the microwave.

3. **Remove the vent cover.** Tip the vent cover forward, then lift it out to remove it from the microwave.

<PIC>

4. **Remove the bulb holder.** After the vent cover is removed, locate the bulb holder inside the opening and remove the screw/fastener holding it in place.

<PIC>

5. **Lift up the bulb holder.** Carefully lift and pull the bulb holder forward so you can access the bulb.

<PIC>

6. **Replace the bulb.** Remove the old bulb and install a new **30-watt or 40-watt appliance bulb**.

7. **Reinstall the bulb holder.** Place the bulb holder back into position and secure it properly.

8. **Reinstall the vent cover.** Slide the bottom of the vent cover into place first, then push the top until it snaps into position. Reinstall the mounting screws.

9. **Restore power.** Plug the microwave back in or turn the power back on at the main power supply.

**Safety note:** To avoid electrical shock, do not remove the outer case of the microwave at any time. The outer case should only be removed by an authorized service technician.,['Manual24_54', 'Manual24_55', 'Manual24_56']

You can configure and use the motherboard’s PCI Express 3.0 x16 slots based on how many VGA/PCIe graphics cards you install. The operating mode is determined mainly by the slot population, and the link speed can also be adjusted in BIOS when needed.

<PIC>

Recommended slot usage and operating speed:

• Single VGA/PCIe card: install the graphics card in the PCIe 3.0 x16_1 slot, which is the gray slot. This slot runs at x16 mode and is recommended for the best performance with one graphics card.

• Dual VGA/PCIe cards: install the cards in PCIe 3.0 x16_1 and PCIe 3.0 x16_2. In this configuration, both slots operate at x8 speed.

• Triple VGA/PCIe cards: install the cards in PCIe 3.0 x16_1, PCIe 3.0 x16_2, and PCIe 3.0 x16_3. In this configuration, PCIe 3.0 x16_1 and PCIe 3.0 x16_2 operate at x8 speed, while PCIe 3.0 x16_3 operates at x4 speed.

If you need to set the PCIe link generation/speed in BIOS, enter BIOS and go to:
Advanced > System Agent (SA) Configuration > PEG Port Configuration

Then adjust:
PCIEx16_1 Link Speed / PCIEx16_2 Link Speed

Available options are:
[Auto] [Gen1] [Gen2] [Gen3]

In most cases, [Auto] is recommended. If you are troubleshooting compatibility with an older PCIe card or riser cable, you may manually select Gen1, Gen2, or Gen3.

Important reminders:

• Always turn off the system and unplug the power cord before installing or removing any expansion card.
• When using multiple graphics cards, make sure the power supply provides sufficient wattage and the required PCIe power connectors.
• For better cooling with multiple graphics cards, connect chassis fans to CHA_FAN1/CHA_FAN2/CHA_FAN3 and ensure good airflow inside the case.,['Manual25_31']

Before changing any motherboard jumper settings, please power down the system completely and follow these precautions:

<PIC>

• Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any component.
• Make sure the ATX power supply is switched off, or disconnect the power cord from the power supply before installing/removing components.
• To avoid electrostatic damage, touch a grounded metal object or use a grounded wrist strap before handling the motherboard.
• Handle components by their edges and avoid touching chips, contacts, or circuitry.

The motherboard has two jumper-related settings you may need to adjust:

1. Clear RTC RAM / Clear CMOS — 2-pin CLRTC

The CLRTC header is used to clear the Real Time Clock (RTC) RAM in CMOS. This resets stored system setup information such as date, time, BIOS settings, and system passwords.

<PIC>

To clear the RTC RAM:
1. Turn off the computer and unplug the power cord.
2. Locate the 2-pin CLRTC header on the motherboard.
3. Use a metal object, such as a screwdriver, to short the two CLRTC pins briefly.
4. Plug the power cord back in and turn on the computer.
5. During startup, hold the <Del> key to enter BIOS setup, then re-enter or adjust the required settings.

Additional notes:
• If the system still does not reset properly, remove the onboard button-cell battery, short the two CLRTC pins again, then reinstall the battery after clearing CMOS.
• If the system fails due to overclocking, you usually do not need to clear RTC manually. Shut down and restart the system; the BIOS can automatically restore safe default parameters through CPU Parameter Recall.

2. CPU Over Voltage jumper — 3-pin CPU_OV

The CPU_OV jumper allows the motherboard to provide a higher CPU voltage range for advanced overclocking, depending on the installed CPU.

<PIC>

Set the CPU_OV jumper as follows:
• Pins 1–2 shorted: CPU Over Voltage disabled — this is the default setting.
• Pins 2–3 shorted: CPU Over Voltage enabled — use this only if you need additional CPU voltage options for overclocking.

Important: Enabling CPU overvoltage may increase heat output and stress on the CPU. Only use this setting if you understand the overclocking requirements and have adequate cooling. If you are not overclocking, keep the jumper on pins 1–2.,['Manual25_5', 'Manual25_33', 'Manual25_34']

If you mean how to identify and use the motherboard’s rear panel connectors, you can refer to the labeled rear I/O layout below. Each number on the image corresponds to a connector function listed after it.

<PIC>

1. **PS/2 Mouse/Keyboard combo port**: Connects a PS/2 mouse or PS/2 keyboard.
2. **DisplayPort**: Connects DisplayPort-compatible monitors or display devices.
3. **VGA port**: Connects a VGA monitor or other VGA-compatible display device.
4. **LAN (RJ-45) port**: Connects the system to a Local Area Network through a network cable, router, switch, or hub.

For the LAN port, the two LEDs indicate connection and speed status:

<PIC>

- **Activity/Link LED**: Shows whether the network is connected and whether data is being transmitted.
- **Speed LED**: Shows the current network speed, such as 10 Mbps, 100 Mbps, or 1 Gbps.

5. **Center/Subwoofer port — Orange**: Connects the center/subwoofer speakers in multi-channel audio setups.
6. **Rear Speaker Out port — Black**: Connects rear speakers for 4.1, 5.1, or 7.1-channel audio.
7. **Line In port — Light Blue**: Connects external audio sources such as a CD/DVD player or other audio input devices.
8. **Line Out port — Lime**: Connects headphones or front speakers. In 4.1/5.1/7.1-channel mode, this works as **Front Speaker Out**.
9. **Microphone port — Pink**: Connects a microphone.

The audio ports may change function depending on whether you are using 2-channel, 4.1-channel, 5.1-channel, or 7.1-channel audio. Please match your speaker setup according to the table below:

<PIC>

10. **Optical S/PDIF Out port**: Connects to amplified speakers, headphones, or S/PDIF-compatible digital audio devices.
11. **USB 3.1 Type-A port**: Connects USB 3.1 Type-A devices.
12. **USB 3.1 Type-C port**: Connects USB 3.1 Type-C mobile devices or peripherals.
13. **USB 3.0 ports 3–6**: Connect USB 3.0 devices. For best speed and stability, USB 3.0 devices should be connected to these USB 3.0 ports.
14. **DVI-D port**: Connects DVI-D-compatible display devices. Please note that DVI-D cannot output an RGB signal to a CRT monitor and is not compatible with DVI-I.
15. **HDMI port**: Connects HDMI display devices and supports HDCP-protected content such as Blu-ray or other protected video playback.
16. **USB 2.0 ports 13 and 14**: Connect USB 2.0 or USB 1.1 devices.

Additional notes:
- For better performance, connect USB 3.0 devices to USB 3.0 ports rather than USB 2.0 ports.
- Multi-display output supports up to three displays in the operating system, two displays in BIOS, and one display in DOS.
- If a rear port does not work, please check that the related controller is enabled in BIOS, such as LAN, USB, or HD Audio, and confirm that the required drivers are installed in the operating system.,['Manual25_35', 'Manual25_36', 'Manual25_38']

You can set the motherboard onboard LED behavior in either of the following ways:

1. From BIOS: go to Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration.
2. From Windows: use the LED Control app in Ai Suite 3.

There are two configurable onboard lighting areas: Model name LEDs and SupremeFX LED. The Standby Power LED is only a status indicator and is not used for lighting mode customization.

Model name LEDs

The Model name LEDs are five small LEDs located below the printed motherboard model name. They are used to highlight the model name area.

<PIC>

To change the Model name LED setting in BIOS:

Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Model Name LED Lighting

Available options:
- Disabled: turns off the Model name LEDs
- Still Mode: keeps the LEDs constantly lit
- Breathing Mode: makes the LEDs blink/fade intermittently

<PIC>

SupremeFX LED

The SupremeFX LED is located along the audio separation area of the motherboard. It provides lighting effects around the onboard audio section.

<PIC>

To change the SupremeFX LED setting in BIOS:

Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > SupremeFX LED Lighting

Available options:
- Disabled: turns off the SupremeFX LED
- Still Mode: keeps the LED constantly lit
- Breathing Mode: makes the LED blink/fade intermittently
- Flowing Mode: creates a flowing lighting effect

<PIC>

Important note about the Standby Power LED

The Standby Power LED is not a decorative lighting option. It is an indicator that lights up when the system is ON, in sleep mode, or in soft-off mode. Its purpose is to remind you that standby power is still present on the motherboard.

Before removing or installing any motherboard component, please shut down the system and unplug the power cable first.

<PIC>,['Manual25_55', 'Manual25_56', 'Manual25_57', 'Manual25_58', 'Manual25_54']

You can update the motherboard BIOS in three ways: **EZ Flash 3**, **EZ Update**, or **BIOS Updater in DOS**. Before starting, we recommend saving a copy of the current BIOS file to a USB flash drive in case you need to restore it later.

## 1. Update BIOS using EZ Flash 3
EZ Flash 3 lets you update the BIOS directly from the BIOS interface, without using an operating-system-based utility. You can update either from a USB flash drive or through the Internet.

<PIC>

### Steps:
1. Enter the BIOS setup program and switch to **Advanced Mode**.
2. Go to the **Tool** menu, select **EZ Flash 3 Utility**, then press **Enter**.
3. Choose one of the update methods:

### Update via USB
1. Prepare a USB flash drive containing the latest BIOS file.
2. Insert the USB flash drive into a USB port.
3. Select **by USB**.
4. Press **Tab** to switch to the **Drive** field.
5. Use the **Up/Down** arrow keys to select the USB flash drive, then press **Enter**.
6. Press **Tab** to switch to the **Folder Info** field.
7. Use the **Up/Down** arrow keys to select the BIOS file, then press **Enter** to start the BIOS update.

### Update via Internet
1. Select **by Internet**.
2. Use the **Left/Right** arrow keys to choose the Internet connection method, then press **Enter**.
3. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the update.

After the update is complete, restart the system.

**Important notes for EZ Flash 3:**
- The USB flash drive must be formatted as **FAT32 or FAT16** and should use a **single partition**.
- Do **not** shut down or reset the system while the BIOS is being updated, as this may cause boot failure.
- After updating, load the BIOS default settings to help ensure system compatibility and stability.

## 2. Update BIOS using EZ Update in AI Suite 3
EZ Update allows you to update motherboard software, drivers, and BIOS more easily from within the operating system. It can automatically check for updates online, or you can manually select a downloaded BIOS file.

<PIC>

### Steps:
1. Open **AI Suite 3**.
2. Click **EZ Update** on the main menu bar.
3. To check online, click **Check Now!**.
4. To update with a downloaded BIOS file, click the browse button, select the BIOS file, then click **Update**.
5. Follow the on-screen instructions to complete the process.

**Note:** EZ Update requires an active Internet connection if you want to check and download updates online.

## 3. Update BIOS using BIOS Updater in DOS
BIOS Updater allows you to update the BIOS in a DOS environment. This method requires a USB flash drive containing the latest BIOS file and the BIOS Updater utility.

Before using this method:
- Prepare a USB flash drive in **FAT32 or FAT16** format with a single partition.
- Save the latest BIOS file and BIOS Updater utility to the USB flash drive.
- NTFS is not supported in the FreeDOS environment.

<PIC>

### Steps:
1. Boot into the FreeDOS environment.
2. At the FreeDOS prompt, type:
   `bupdater /pc /g`
   Then press **Enter**.
3. On the BIOS Updater screen, press **Tab** to switch from the **Files** panel to the **Drives** panel.
4. Select the USB drive, usually shown as **D:**.
5. Press **Tab** again to switch back to the **Files** panel.
6. Use the **Up/Down** or **Home/End** keys to select the BIOS file, then press **Enter**.
7. After BIOS Updater checks the selected file, confirm the update when prompted.

<PIC>

8. Select **Yes**, then press **Enter** to start the update.
9. When the BIOS update is complete, press **Esc** to exit BIOS Updater.
10. Restart the computer.

**Important:** Do not shut down or reset the system during the BIOS update process. After updating, load the optimized default BIOS settings to help maintain system stability and compatibility.,['Manual25_61', 'Manual25_60', 'Manual25_63', 'Manual25_64']

To create a RAID array on this motherboard, please follow the steps below. Before starting, back up any important data on the drives, as creating RAID may erase the disk contents.

1. Connect your HDDs/SSDs to the Intel SATA 6.0 Gb/s connectors on the motherboard. These are the SATA6G_1~6 / SATA Express related Intel chipset ports shown below.

<PIC>

Important notes before setup:
• Use Intel SATA connectors for the drives that will be included in the RAID array.
• Make sure the drives do not already contain an existing RAID volume.
• The SATA connectors are set to AHCI by default. If RAID is not enabled automatically through the wizard, enter BIOS and set SATA Mode Selection to RAID.

2. Enter BIOS, then open EZ Tuning Wizard. You can press <F11> on the keyboard or click EZ Tuning Wizard from the BIOS EZ Mode screen.

<PIC>

3. In EZ Tuning Wizard, select the RAID tab, then click Yes to enable RAID mode.

<PIC>

4. The available drives will be displayed. Confirm that the disks you want to use are listed, then click Next to continue.

<PIC>

5. Select the storage purpose for your RAID setup, then click Next:
• Easy Backup: focuses on data redundancy/protection.
• Super Speed: focuses on higher performance.

<PIC>

6. If you selected Easy Backup, choose one of the following options, then click Next:
• Easy Backup (RAID1): mirrors data between drives for redundancy.
• Easy Backup (RAID10): combines mirroring and striping; this option is available only when four HDDs/SSDs are connected.

<PIC>

7. If you selected Super Speed, choose one of the following options, then click Next:
• Super Speed (RAID0): improves performance by striping data across drives, but does not provide redundancy.
• Super Speed (RAID5): provides a balance of performance and redundancy, and requires enough drives to support RAID5.

<PIC>

8. After selecting the RAID type, click Yes to continue with the RAID setup.

9. When the RAID setup is complete, click Yes to exit the setup, then click OK to restart the system.

After the restart, the RAID array should be created and ready for OS installation or storage use, depending on your configuration.,['Manual25_48', 'Manual25_65', 'Manual25_71', 'Manual25_72', 'Manual25_73', 'Manual25_74', 'Manual25_75']

You can install OS 7 on a 100 Series motherboard by using a SATA optical drive together with a USB installation device. This method lets the system load the required USB 3.0 driver automatically during the installation startup.

**Preparation**

Please prepare the following items:

- 1 x motherboard support DVD
- 1 x OS 7 installation source
- 1 x SATA optical disc drive (SATA ODD)
- 1 x USB device, such as a USB optical disc drive or USB storage device

If you use a USB storage device, it should have **8 GB or more capacity**. We recommend formatting the USB storage device before creating or copying the installation files.

**Installation steps**

1. Prepare the OS 7 installation media:
   - If using a USB optical disc drive, insert the OS 7 installation DVD into the USB ODD.
   - If using a USB storage device, copy all files from the OS 7 installation DVD to the USB storage device on another working computer.

2. Connect the USB ODD or USB storage device to the 100 Series motherboard system.

3. Insert the motherboard support DVD into the SATA ODD connected to the same system.

4. Power on the system. During POST, press **F8** to open the boot device selection screen.

<PIC>

5. In the boot menu, select the USB ODD or USB storage device that contains the OS 7 installation files, then press **Enter** to boot from it.

6. During the installation startup, the USB 3.0 driver will be loaded automatically from the support DVD in the SATA ODD. If the driver loads correctly, you will see the **“Setup is starting...”** screen.

<PIC>

7. After the setup screen appears, follow the on-screen instructions to complete the OS 7 installation.

If the USB keyboard, mouse, or installation USB device is not detected during setup, please confirm that the support DVD is inserted in the SATA ODD and that the USB device is properly connected before restarting the installation process.,['Manual25_87', 'Manual25_88']

To secure the motherboard to the chassis, first make sure the power cord is unplugged and the system is fully powered off to avoid injury or damage to components.

Place the motherboard into the chassis in the correct orientation, with the side containing the external I/O ports facing the rear of the chassis. Then align the motherboard’s screw holes with the chassis standoffs.

Insert and fasten nine screws into the screw holes marked by the red circles in the image below:

<PIC>

Tighten the screws until the motherboard is firmly secured, but do not over-tighten them. Excessive force may bend or damage the motherboard.,['Manual25_6']

To prepare the motherboard for installing the Central Processing Unit (CPU), first confirm the CPU socket type and follow the required safety checks before handling the processor or socket.

<PIC>

This motherboard uses a surface-mount LGA1151 CPU socket, designed for 6th Generation processors. Before installing the CPU, please prepare as follows:

1. Disconnect all power first  
Unplug all power cables from the system before installing the CPU. This helps prevent electrical damage to the motherboard, CPU, and other components.

2. Verify CPU compatibility  
Install only a CPU designed for the LGA1151 socket. Do not install CPUs designed for LGA1150, LGA1155, or LGA1156 sockets, as they are not compatible and may damage the socket contacts.

3. Check the CPU socket condition  
Before installation, make sure the PnP protective cap is still installed on the CPU socket and that the socket contacts are not bent or damaged. If the cap is missing, or if you notice damage to the PnP cap, socket contacts, or motherboard components, please contact your retailer immediately.

4. Keep the PnP cap after installation  
After the CPU is installed, keep the PnP cap in a safe place. It may be required if the motherboard needs to be returned for service, as the socket should be protected with the cap.

5. Avoid socket contact damage  
CPU socket contact damage caused by incorrect CPU installation or removal, incorrect handling of the PnP cap, or loss of the cap may not be covered by warranty.

Before touching the motherboard or any internal component, also follow these handling precautions:

<PIC>

• Unplug the power cord from the wall socket before touching any component.  
• Use a grounded wrist strap, or touch a safely grounded metal object such as the power supply case, to prevent static electricity damage.  
• Hold the CPU and other components by their edges; avoid touching pins, contacts, chips, or circuitry.  
• If you remove any component, place it on a grounded antistatic pad or in its antistatic bag.  
• Before installing or removing any component, ensure the ATX power supply is switched off or the power cord is detached from the power supply.

Once these checks are complete, the motherboard is ready for CPU installation.,['Manual25_9', 'Manual25_5']

You can check the system memory information of this motherboard in three practical ways: by confirming the memory specifications, checking the physical DIMM slots, and viewing the detected memory in BIOS.

1. Check the motherboard memory specifications

This motherboard supports DDR4 system memory. The key specifications are:

- Memory type: DDR4 DIMM
- Number of memory slots: 4 DIMM slots
- Maximum supported capacity: up to 64 GB
- Supported memory frequency: DDR4 2133 MHz up to DDR4 3400 MHz when overclocked
- Supported module type: unbuffered, non-ECC DDR4 DIMMs

<PIC>

2. Check the memory slots on the motherboard

The motherboard has four DDR4 DIMM sockets. The slots are labeled:

- DIMM_A1
- DIMM_A2
- DIMM_B1
- DIMM_B2

These labels help you identify where the memory modules are installed and whether they are placed correctly for single-channel or dual-channel operation. For better compatibility, if you install only one memory module, DIMM_A2 is recommended. If you install two memory modules, DIMM_A2 and DIMM_B2 are recommended.

<PIC>

Important compatibility notes:

- Only DDR4 memory modules should be installed in these slots.
- Do not install DDR, DDR2, or DDR3 modules into DDR4 slots, as they are physically and electrically different.
- DIMM voltage below 1.65 V is recommended to help protect the CPU.

3. Check the installed memory in BIOS

You can also confirm the actual memory detected by the system through BIOS.

Steps:

1. Start or restart the computer.
2. Enter BIOS during boot.
3. Go to Advanced Mode.
4. Open the Main menu.
5. Check the basic system information area, where memory information is displayed.

<PIC>

For more detailed memory module information, you can also go to:

Tool menu > SPD Information

There you can select each memory slot to view the SPD information of the installed DIMM module. Available slot options include:

- DIMM_A1
- DIMM_B1
- DIMM_A2
- DIMM_B2

So, in short: the motherboard’s supported memory can be checked from its specifications, the installed memory position can be checked from the DIMM slot labels, and the currently detected memory capacity/details can be checked in BIOS.,['Manual25_2', 'Manual25_18', 'Manual25_78']

The Serial port connector, labeled as the 10-1 pin COM header, is an internal motherboard connector used to add a traditional serial/COM port to the system.

To use it, connect the cable from a compatible serial port module to the COM header on the motherboard, then secure the module’s DB-9 serial port bracket to an available slot opening at the rear of the chassis.

<PIC>

In the diagram, you can see both the COM header location on the motherboard and the pin layout. The connector uses a 10-1 pin design, with one missing pin used as a key to help align the cable correctly. The main signal pins include DCD, RXD, TXD, DTR, GND, DSR, RTS, CTS, and RI.

Please note: the COM/serial port module is not included with the motherboard and needs to be purchased separately if you require a physical DB-9 serial port.

You can also enable or configure the serial port in BIOS through the following path:

Advanced > Onboard Devices Configuration > Serial Port Configuration

Available options include:

- Serial Port [On/Off]: enables or disables the onboard COM port.
- Change Settings: appears only when Serial Port is set to [On], and lets you select the base address and IRQ.

Supported base address options are:

- IO=3F8h; IRQ=4
- IO=2F8h; IRQ=3
- IO=3E8h; IRQ=4
- IO=2E8h; IRQ=3

For installation, make sure the system is powered off and unplugged before connecting the module cable, and align the connector carefully to avoid incorrect pin connection.,['Manual25_39']

The TPM connector on this motherboard is a 14-1 pin internal header used to connect a Trusted Platform Module (TPM). A TPM helps provide hardware-based security by securely storing items such as encryption keys, digital certificates, passwords, and other sensitive data. It can also help improve network security, protect digital identities, and verify platform integrity.

<PIC>

In the diagram, the TPM header is shown on the bottom edge of the motherboard, with a close-up of the 14-1 pin layout. The pin assignments are as follows:

- Top row: F_CLKRUN, F_SERIRQ, F_FRAME#, F_LAD3, F_LAD2, F_LAD1, F_LAD0
- Bottom row: +3VSB, S_PCIRST#_TBD, key space, GND, C_PCICLK_TPM, +3V, +3V

The missing/key pin position helps ensure the TPM module is aligned correctly, so please match the TPM module connector orientation with the header before installation.

Important: Before installing or removing the TPM module or any other motherboard component, turn off the computer and unplug the power cord. This helps prevent damage to the motherboard, the TPM module, or other components.,['Manual25_40']

The Thermal sensor connector, labeled as the 2-pin T_SENSOR / T_SENSOR1 header on the motherboard, is used to connect a thermistor cable. After connected, the thermistor can measure the temperature of a specific area or device inside the system, such as a motherboard hotspot, graphics card area, storage device area, or other component you want to monitor.

<PIC>

As shown above, the T_SENSOR1 header has 2 pins:
- Pin 1: SENSOR IN
- Pin 2: GND

Please connect the thermistor cable according to the pin orientation. The thermistor cable is not included with the motherboard and needs to be purchased separately.

After installation, you can check the detected temperature in BIOS under the Monitor menu. The reading will appear as T_Sensor Temperature. If you do not want this temperature item to be monitored or displayed, you can set it to [Ignore].

<PIC>

This sensor reading can also be used as a temperature source for fan control. For example, chassis fans or extension fans can be configured to adjust speed based on T_Sensor temperature, which is useful if you want fans to react to the temperature of a specific component or area inside the case.

Important safety note: before connecting or removing the thermistor cable, please shut down the system and unplug the power cord to avoid possible electrical damage or injury.,['Manual25_43', 'Manual25_83']

To set **Quick Release (QR/QPR)** on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer after pressure cooking, follow these steps:

1. **Wait until the cooking program has finished.**  
   Once the program ends, keep the pressure cooking lid in place.

2. **Press the quick release button down firmly** until it **clicks and locks into the Vent position**.

<PIC>

3. **Allow the steam to release.**  
   After the button is pressed down, a strong jet of hot steam will come out from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal during Quick Release.

<PIC>

4. **Wait for the float valve to drop.**  
   When all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid. Only then is it safe to open the lid.

The quick release button controls the steam release valve:
- **Seal position:** the button is popped up.
- **Vent position:** the button is pushed down.

<PIC>

Important safety reminders:
- Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam release valve while steam is venting.
- Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve.
- Do not try to remove or force open the lid while the float valve is still up.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods such as stews, chilis, pasta, or congee, or for foods that expand during cooking such as beans and grains. For these foods, use Natural Release instead.,['multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02', 'Manual30_14']

To set the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, use the pressure cooking lid—not the air fryer lid—for pressure cooking functions such as Pressure Cook, Steam, Slow Cook, Sauté, and Sous Vide.

The pressure cooking lid is designed to automatically set itself to “Seal” when it is properly closed. You may also hear a jingle when the lid is opened or closed. During non-pressure cooking, the lid can be removed; however, once the cooker becomes pressurized, the lid locks for safety and cannot be removed until pressure has fully released.

<PIC>

To close and set the pressure cooking lid:
1. Hold the lid by the handle.
2. Align the open-position symbol on the lid with the matching symbol on the cooker base.
3. Lower the lid onto the lid track.
4. Turn the lid clockwise until the close/lock symbol on the lid aligns with the matching symbol on the cooker base.
5. Once correctly positioned, the lid should sit securely and the cooker will be ready to build pressure when a pressure cooking program is started.

<PIC>

To remove the pressure cooking lid after cooking:
1. Make sure all pressure has been released and the float valve has dropped down.
2. Grip the lid handle firmly.
3. Turn the lid counterclockwise until the open-position symbol on the lid aligns with the symbol on the rim of the cooker base.
4. Lift the lid up and away from the cooker base, tilting it toward your body carefully so steam and condensation move away from you.

<PIC>

Important safety note: Never try to open or force the lid while the float valve is raised. A raised float valve means the cooker is still pressurized. Wait until the float valve drops completely before attempting to turn or remove the lid.,['Manual30_11', 'Manual30_13', 'Manual30_12']

To set/install the float valve on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please use the pressure cooking lid and follow these steps:

1. Insert the float valve from the top of the lid

Drop the narrow end of the float valve into the float valve hole on the top side of the pressure cooking lid. Then place one finger on the flat top of the float valve to hold it in place.

<PIC>

2. Turn the lid over and secure the silicone cap

While keeping the float valve in position, turn the lid over. Firmly attach the silicone cap onto the bottom end of the float valve. Make sure the cap is fully seated so the float valve cannot fall out.

<PIC>

3. Check before use

Before pressure cooking, confirm that both parts are properly installed:
- The float valve is inserted through the lid.
- The silicone cap is securely attached underneath.
- The float valve can move up and down freely.

Please do not operate the pressure cooker if the float valve or silicone cap is missing or not installed correctly.

Important safety reminder: Do not touch the float valve while the cooker is in use. If the float valve is raised, the cooker is still pressurized. Do not try to remove or force open the lid until the float valve has dropped back down.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The anti-block shield is an important safety part on the pressure cooking lid of your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Its job is to help prevent food particles from entering the steam release pipe, which supports proper pressure regulation during pressure cooking. Because it helps keep the steam release system clear, it must be installed whenever you use the pressure cooking function.

<PIC>

Before each pressure-cooking use, please check that the steam release valve, steam release pipe, anti-block shield, and float valve are clean and not clogged. This is especially important after cooking foods that can foam, expand, or leave residue, such as grains, beans, pasta, oatmeal, soups, stews, or thick sauces.

To remove the anti-block shield for cleaning:
1. Make sure the lid is completely cool and removed from the cooker base.
2. Hold the lid firmly, similar to holding a steering wheel.
3. Use your thumbs to press firmly against the side of the anti-block shield, pushing toward the side of the lid and upward.
4. Continue pressing until the shield pops off the prongs underneath.

<PIC>

To reinstall the anti-block shield:
1. Position the anti-block shield directly over the prongs on the underside of the lid.
2. Press it down firmly until it snaps securely into place.
3. Confirm it is seated properly before pressure cooking.

<PIC>

Please do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed. After each use, clean the pressure cooking lid and removable parts, then allow all surfaces to dry thoroughly before using or storing the appliance.,['Manual30_19', 'Manual30_34', 'Manual30_35']

The steam release valve is controlled by the quick release button on the pressure cooking lid. This button determines whether the cooker stays sealed to build/hold pressure, or vents steam to release pressure.

<PIC>

To set the valve, use the quick release button as follows:

1. Seal position — for pressure cooking / natural pressure release
   - Leave the quick release button popped up.
   - When the pressure cooking lid is closed, it is designed to set itself to Seal automatically.
   - If you need to re-seat it, use only a small flicking motion to set it back to Seal.
   - Do not rotate the quick release button more than 45°.

2. Vent position — for quick pressure release
   - Press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.
   - This opens the steam release valve so pressure can escape.

<PIC>

When the button is locked in Vent, steam will release upward through the top of the steam release valve. This is normal during quick release.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Do not cover, block, or place anything over the steam release valve.
- Keep hands, face, and exposed skin away from the valve while steam is releasing.
- Do not try to open the lid while the float valve is still raised.
- Only open the lid after the float valve has dropped, indicating the cooker has fully depressurized.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

To install the anti-block shield on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can see the underside of the lid.
2. Locate the metal prongs near the steam release pipe.
3. Position the anti-block shield directly over the prongs.
4. Press the shield down firmly until it snaps securely into place.

<PIC>

Before pressure cooking, always check that the anti-block shield is properly installed and not clogged with food debris. Do not operate the pressure cooker without the anti-block shield installed.

The anti-block shield helps stop food particles from entering the steam release pipe, which supports proper pressure regulation during cooking. It is an important safety component and is required for pressure cooking.

<PIC>,['Manual30_35', 'Manual30_19']

To set the float valve and silicone cap on the pressure cooking lid, please follow these steps:

1. **Insert the float valve from the top of the lid**  
   Hold the pressure cooking lid with the top side facing up. Drop the **narrow end** of the float valve into the float valve hole on the top of the lid. The flat top of the float valve should remain visible on the lid surface.

<PIC>

2. **Secure the silicone cap from the underside**  
   Place one finger on the flat top of the float valve to keep it from falling out, then carefully turn the lid over. From the underside of the lid, firmly push the silicone cap onto the bottom of the float valve until it is securely attached.

<PIC>

3. **Check before use**  
   Make sure both the float valve and silicone cap are properly installed before pressure cooking. The float valve and silicone cap work together to seal in pressurized steam, so the cooker should not be operated if either part is missing, loose, or incorrectly fitted.

**Important safety reminder:** Do not touch the float valve during cooking. If the float valve is raised, the cooker is still pressurized—do not try to open the lid until the float valve has dropped back down.,['Manual30_36', 'Manual30_37']

The condensation collector is installed on the back of the cooker base. Its purpose is to catch any liquid that may overflow from the condensation rim during cooking.

To install it:

1. Locate the tabs on the back of the cooker base.
2. Hold the condensation collector with its grooves facing the tabs.
3. Align the grooves on the collector over the tabs on the cooker base.
4. Slide the condensation collector straight into place until it sits securely.

<PIC>

Please make sure the condensation collector is installed before cooking. After each use, remove it, empty any collected liquid, rinse it out, and let it dry before reinstalling.,['Manual30_38']

To install the sealing ring on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the pressure cooking lid over so you can access the underside of the lid.
2. Position the silicone sealing ring around the circular sealing ring rack.
3. Press the sealing ring firmly into place all the way around the rack. Make sure it sits evenly and does not pucker, twist, or bulge.

<PIC>

4. Check the fit after installation: the sealing ring should sit snugly behind the sealing ring rack.
5. Turn the lid over gently to confirm the sealing ring stays in place. If it falls out or looks uneven, remove it and reinstall it.

Important safety checks before cooking:
- Use only authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cracked, cut, deformed, or damaged.
- Always confirm the sealing ring is installed correctly before pressure cooking.
- Sealing rings naturally stretch over time. Replace the sealing ring every 12–18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, damage, or poor sealing performance.,['Manual30_31']

To remove the silicone sealing ring from your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer, please follow these steps:

1. **Turn the appliance off and unplug it.** Make sure the cooker has completely cooled to room temperature before handling or cleaning any parts.

2. **Remove the pressure cooking lid and turn it over.** You should be looking at the underside of the lid, where the silicone sealing ring sits around the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack.

3. **Grip the edge of the silicone sealing ring.** Gently pull the ring outward and away from behind the stainless-steel sealing ring rack. Work around the lid as needed until the entire ring comes free.

<PIC>

4. **Inspect the sealing ring rack after removal.** Check that the stainless-steel rack is centered, secure, and sitting at an even height all the way around the lid. If the rack looks bent, loose, uneven, or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

**Tip:** The sealing ring is usually easier to remove and reinstall when it is wet and soapy.

**Important safety note:** Only use authorized sealing rings designed for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cut, cracked, warped, or otherwise damaged. Before each pressure-cooking use, always check that the sealing ring is clean, undamaged, and correctly seated behind the rack.,['Manual30_30']

The sealing ring is a key part of the pressure cooking lid on your multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. When the pressure cooking lid is closed, the sealing ring forms an air-tight seal between the lid and the inner pot, allowing pressure to build properly during pressure cooking.

The sealing ring must be installed before using any pressure cooking function. Please make sure only one sealing ring is installed in the lid at a time.

<PIC>

A helpful tip: the sealing ring is made of silicone, and silicone can absorb strong smells and flavors. If you cook both savory and sweet dishes, or strongly scented foods, it’s a good idea to keep extra sealing rings and use separate ones to help reduce flavor transfer between dishes.

Important safety notes:
- Only use authorized sealing rings made for this multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer.
- Do not use a sealing ring that is stretched, cut, deformed, or damaged.
- Before each pressure cooking use, check that the sealing ring is clean, correctly installed, and free from cuts or deformation.
- Sealing rings naturally stretch over time. Replace the sealing ring every 12–18 months, or sooner if you notice stretching, deformation, cracking, or damage.

To remove the sealing ring:
Grip the edge of the silicone ring and pull it out from behind the circular stainless-steel sealing ring rack on the underside of the pressure cooking lid.

<PIC>

After removing it, check the stainless-steel sealing ring rack to make sure it is centered, secure, and even all the way around. If the rack looks bent or deformed, do not try to repair it yourself.

To install the sealing ring:
Place the sealing ring over the sealing ring rack, then press it firmly into place all the way around. Make sure it sits smoothly behind the rack with no puckering, twisting, or loose sections. When installed correctly, the sealing ring should fit snugly and should not fall out when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

If you notice steam escaping from the sides of the lid during pressure cooking, turn the appliance off and allow it to depressurize safely before opening. Then check that the sealing ring is properly installed, clean, and not damaged. Using the cooker with an incorrectly installed or damaged sealing ring may cause food or steam to discharge, which can lead to injury or property damage.,['Manual30_16', 'Manual30_30', 'Manual30_31']

The steam release valve is an important pressure-control and safety component on the multi-use pressure cooker and air fryer. It is located on the top of the pressure cooking lid, close to the float valve and the quick release button.

<PIC>

The valve sits on the steam release pipe and is designed to release steam upward when pressure is being vented. It may feel a little loose when installed correctly—this is normal. However, it must still stay in place if the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

For safe pressure cooking, the steam release valve must be installed and unobstructed. Please do not cover, block, or place anything over the valve. Blocking it can prevent proper pressure release and may create a safety risk.

The quick release button controls whether the steam release valve is in the sealing or venting state. In the Seal position, pressure is retained for pressure cooking. In the Vent position, pressure is released through the steam release valve.

<PIC>

To perform a Quick Release, press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks into the Vent position.

<PIC>

Once the button is pressed down, a strong jet of hot steam will come out from the top of the steam release valve. This is expected during Quick Release. Keep your hands, face, and exposed skin away from the steam path.

<PIC>

For cleaning or checking the part, you can remove the steam release valve by pulling it straight up and off the steam release pipe. The cooker should not be used for pressure cooking unless the steam release valve is properly installed.

<PIC>

To reinstall it, place the steam release valve back onto the steam release pipe and press down firmly. When fitted correctly, it will sit loosely on the pipe but should not fall off when the lid is turned over.

<PIC>

A few key safety reminders:
- Do not lean over the steam release valve while the cooker is operating or still under pressure.
- Do not place hands, face, cloths, utensils, or any objects over the steam release valve.
- Before pressure cooking, check that the steam release valve and steam release pipe are clean and not clogged.
- If steam escapes from the steam release valve in a steady stream for longer than about 3 minutes during cooking, turn the appliance off and allow it to depressurize safely before checking it.
- Always wait until pressure has fully released and the float valve has dropped before opening the lid.,['Manual30_3', 'Manual30_15', 'Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02', 'Manual30_32', 'Manual30_33']

The quick release button is the control used to operate the steam release valve on the pressure cooking lid. In simple terms, it determines whether the cooker keeps pressure sealed inside or releases pressure through the valve.

<PIC>

It has two main positions:

- **Seal position**: the quick release button is popped up. Use this position during pressure cooking to keep pressure inside the cooker.
- **Vent position**: press the quick release button down until it clicks and locks. This opens the steam release valve so pressure can be released quickly.

<PIC>

When you press the button down to the Vent position, hot steam will eject from the top of the steam release valve. This is normal during Quick Release. Once all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid, indicating that it is safe to open the lid.

<PIC>

A few useful things to know:

- The quick release button is designed to keep your hands farther away from the steam compared with manually turning a steam valve.
- Although the lid may indicate “Turn to Seal,” the motion is more like a quick flick back to Seal rather than a full turn.
- Do not turn the quick release button more than about 45°. Let it spring back to its original position.

Important safety reminders:

- Do not place your hands, face, or exposed skin over the steam release valve when releasing pressure.
- Do not cover or block the steam release valve.
- Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still raised.
- Do not use Quick Release for fatty, oily, thick, or high-starch foods such as stews, chilis, pasta, congee, or for foods that expand during cooking such as beans and grains. For these foods, Natural Release is usually the safer choice to help prevent splattering or clogging.,['Manual30_14', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_01', 'multi-use_pressure_cooker_and_air_fryer_02']

Natural Release, also called NR or NPR, is a pressure-release method used after pressure cooking. With Natural Release, cooking does not stop instantly; instead, the cooker cools down gradually, and the internal pressure decreases on its own over time.

<PIC>

Here’s what you should know:

1. What to do after cooking finishes
- Leave the quick release button in the popped-up Seal position.
- Turn Keep Warm off, if you do not want extra heat to extend the resting/carryover cooking time.
- Do not press the quick release button or force steam out during Natural Release.

2. What happens during Natural Release
- The temperature inside the cooker slowly drops.
- The pressure reduces naturally without active venting.
- The food continues to cook slightly during this period. This is often called carryover cooking or resting.
- The depressurization time can vary depending on the amount and type of food inside, and it may take up to about 40 minutes.
- Once all pressure has been released, the float valve will drop down into the lid. This indicates the cooker is no longer pressurized.

3. When Natural Release is recommended
Natural Release is especially suitable for foods that may foam, expand, or splatter if pressure is released too quickly, such as:
- Soups, stews, and chilis
- Pasta, oatmeal, and congee
- Beans, grains, and other foods that expand during cooking
- High-starch or thick foods

4. Important safety reminder
Do not try to remove the lid while the float valve is still up. If the float valve is raised, the cooker is still pressurized. Wait until the float valve drops completely before opening the lid, and never force the lid open.,['Manual30_9']

The robot vacuum’s anatomy can be understood in three main areas: the top view, the control buttons and indicators, and the bottom view.

**Top View**
<PIC>
From the top, the main visible parts are:
- **iAdapt® Localization Camera**: Helps the robot navigate and localize itself while cleaning.
- **Handle**: Used for lifting or carrying the robot when needed.
- **Bin Release Button**: Press this button to remove the dust bin for emptying.

**Buttons & Indicators**
<PIC>
The control panel includes the main operating buttons and status indicators:
- **DOCK Button**: Sends the robot back to its charging base.
- **CLEAN Button**: Starts, pauses, or resumes a cleaning cycle.
- **SPOT Button**: Activates spot cleaning for a concentrated area of debris.
- **Wi‑Fi® Indicator**: Shows Wi‑Fi connection status.
- **Troubleshooting Indicator**: Alerts you when the robot needs attention.
- **Battery Indicator**: Shows battery or charging status.
- **Full Bin Indicator**: Lights up when the dust bin needs to be emptied.
- **Dirt Detect™ Indicator**: Indicates when the robot has detected a dirtier area and is cleaning it more thoroughly.

**Bottom View**
<PIC>
The underside contains the main cleaning and charging components:
- **Debris Extractors**: Pick up dirt, dust, and debris from the floor.
- **Extractor Caps**: End caps on the extractors that can be removed for cleaning hair or debris buildup.
- **Extractor Frame**: Holds the debris extractors in place.
- **Extractor Frame Release Tabs**: Used to open the extractor frame for maintenance.
- **Dust Bin**: Collects dirt and debris during cleaning.
- **Side Brush**: Sweeps debris from edges and corners into the robot’s cleaning path.
- **Charging Contacts**: Connect with the charging base to recharge the robot.
- **Floor Tracking Sensor**: Helps the robot track movement and navigate across the floor.,['Manual32_1', 'Manual32_2', 'Manual32_3']

The vacuum’s Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier offers two primary modes to help tailor cleaning to different areas of your home:

1. **Halo Mode** — Use this when you want to protect specific objects or small areas.
   - Set the switch to the **down** position.
   - The device creates an invisible circular protection zone that the vacuum will not enter.
   - This is useful for keeping the vacuum away from items such as pet bowls, vases, delicate objects, corners, or areas under furniture.
   - The protected zone extends approximately **24 inches from the center of the device**, creating about a **4-foot diameter** barrier.

<PIC>

2. **Virtual Wall Mode** — Use this when you want to block the vacuum from entering another room or crossing an opening.
   - Set the switch to the **up** position.
   - The device creates an invisible, cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum detects.
   - It can block openings up to approximately **10 feet**, making it suitable for doorways, hallways, or room boundaries.
   - The barrier becomes wider as it extends farther from the device.

<PIC>

You can choose the mode directly using the switch on the Dual Mode Virtual Wall Barrier: **up for Virtual Wall Mode**, **down for Halo Mode**, and the middle position turns the device off.

<PIC>,['Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6', 'Manual32_4']

To empty the vacuum cleaner’s bin, please follow these steps:

1. **Remove the dust bin**  
   Press the **bin release button** on the vacuum, then pull the bin out from the unit as shown below.

<PIC>

2. **Empty the debris**  
   Hold the bin over a trash can, open the bin door, and let the dirt and debris fall out completely.

<PIC>

3. **Reinstall the bin**  
   After emptying it, close the bin door securely and slide the bin back into the vacuum until it is properly seated.

For best performance, we recommend emptying the bin after each use and cleaning the filter regularly. If the **full bin indicator** turns on during a cleaning cycle, you can pause the vacuum, empty the bin, reinstall it, and then resume cleaning.,['Manual32_8', 'Manual32_9']

To clean the vacuum cleaner filter properly, please follow these steps:

1. **Remove the filter**  
   Open the filter compartment and pull the filter out by holding the **yellow tab**. This tab is designed to help you remove the filter easily without touching the dusty surface directly.

   <PIC>

2. **Shake off loose dust and debris**  
   Hold the filter over a trash container, then gently tap it against the inside of the container to remove trapped dust and debris. Avoid washing the filter with water unless specifically directed for your model.

   <PIC>

3. **Reinsert the filter correctly**  
   After cleaning, place the filter back into the compartment with the **yellow tab facing up**. Please make sure the filter is fully seated, as the filter door will not close properly unless the filter is reinstalled.

**Recommended filter care frequency:**
- Clean the filter **once per week**.
- If you have pets, clean it **twice per week**.
- Replace the filter approximately **every 2 months** to maintain strong suction and cleaning performance.,['Manual32_10', 'Manual32_11']

The proper procedure for cleaning the full bin sensors is as follows:

1. Remove the dust bin from the vacuum and empty it completely. This helps ensure dust or debris does not interfere with the sensor cleaning process.

<PIC>

2. With the bin removed, locate the full bin sensors inside the vacuum’s bin compartment. Gently wipe the sensors using a clean, dry cloth. Do not use water, sprays, or cleaning solutions on the sensors.

<PIC>

3. Next, clean the sensor ports on the dust bin itself. Wipe both the inner sensor ports and the outer sensor ports with a clean, dry cloth so the sensors can detect the bin status accurately.

<PIC>

For best performance, clean the full bin sensors once every 2 weeks. Also, if the full bin indicator is on but the bin does not appear to be full, cleaning these sensors and sensor ports is the recommended first step.,['Manual32_12', 'Manual32_13', 'Manual32_14']

To clean the vacuum’s extractors/rollers correctly, please follow these steps:

1. **Open the extractor frame**  
   Turn the vacuum over. Pinch the yellow extractor frame release tabs, then lift the extractor frame upward. Remove any visible obstructions from the frame area.

   <PIC>

2. **Remove the extractors and clean the end caps**  
   Take out both extractors/rollers. Remove the yellow extractor caps from the ends, then clear away any hair, string, dust, or debris collected under the caps and around the metal axles. After cleaning, reinstall the extractor caps securely.

   <PIC>

3. **Clean the opposite ends of the extractors**  
   Check the square and hexagonal plastic pegs on the other side of the extractors. Pull off any wrapped hair or debris so the rollers can rotate smoothly.

   <PIC>

4. **Clear the vacuum path**  
   With the extractors removed, check the open extractor compartment and vacuum path. Remove any trapped debris that could block suction or interfere with roller movement.

   <PIC>

5. **Reinstall the extractors**  
   Place the extractors back into the cleaning head module. Make sure the color and shape of each extractor peg match the corresponding color and shape icons on the cleaning head. Then close the extractor frame until it is secure.

**Maintenance recommendation:** Clean the extractors about once every 4 months, or once every 3 months if you have pets. For best performance, replace the extractors every 6–12 months, depending on wear and usage.,['Manual32_19', 'Manual32_20', 'Manual32_21', 'Manual32_22']

To clean the sensors and charging contacts properly, please follow these steps:

1. **Clean the sensors**
   - Turn the vacuum over and locate the sensors around the underside edge, including the floor tracking sensor.
   - Wipe each sensor gently with a **clean, dry cloth**.
   - Do **not** spray cleaning solution directly onto the sensors or into any sensor openings, as liquid may damage internal components.

<PIC>

2. **Clean the charging contacts**
   - Wipe the charging contacts on the vacuum with a **clean, dry cloth**.
   - Also wipe the charging contacts on the Home Base/dock so both contact points remain clean and can charge reliably.

<PIC>

**Recommended cleaning frequency:** Clean the cliff sensors, charging contacts, iAdapt localization camera, floor tracking sensor, front caster wheel, and side brush **once per month** to help maintain good performance.

**Safety reminder:** Before cleaning or maintenance, always remove the vacuum from the Home Base/dock and make sure your hands are dry. Do not handle the vacuum with wet hands.,['Manual32_17', 'Manual32_18']

To position the Home Base correctly, place it in an open, uncluttered area where the vacuum can easily find and dock with it.

Please make sure the surrounding space meets these requirements:

- Leave at least 1.5 ft / 0.5 m of clear space on each side of the Home Base.
- Leave at least 4 ft / 1.2 m of clear space in front of the Home Base.
- Keep the Home Base at least 4 ft / 1.2 m away from stairs.
- Keep it at least 8 ft / 2.4 m away from any virtual wall barriers.

Also, keep the Home Base plugged in at all times so the vacuum can charge and return to it properly. For best app connectivity and cleaning performance, place the Home Base in an area with stable Wi-Fi coverage.

When placing the vacuum on the Home Base, make sure the charging contacts on the vacuum align with the charging contacts on the dock, as shown below:

<PIC>,['Manual32_18']

To clean the front caster wheel, please follow these steps:

1. Turn the vacuum over and locate the front caster wheel. Pull the front wheel straight out firmly to remove the wheel assembly from its housing.

<PIC>

2. Check the wheel cavity and remove any dirt, dust, hair, or debris from inside the opening.

3. Spin the wheel by hand to confirm it rotates freely. If the wheel feels stuck or does not spin smoothly, remove the wheel from the housing and push firmly to take out the axle. Then clear away any hair or debris wrapped around the axle or inside the wheel.

4. After cleaning, reinstall the axle, wheel, and housing parts. Push the wheel assembly back into place until it clicks securely.

For best performance, clean the front caster wheel about once per month, or more often if hair or debris builds up quickly.,['Manual32_15']

To clean the side brush, please follow these steps:

1. **Disconnect the vacuum first.**  
   Before cleaning or maintaining the vacuum, make sure it is disconnected from power to ensure safe handling.

2. **Turn the vacuum over and locate the side brush.**  
   The side brush is on the underside of the vacuum. Use a coin or a small screwdriver to loosen and remove the screw in the center of the side brush, as shown below:

   <PIC>

3. **Remove and clean the side brush.**  
   Lift the brush off after removing the screw. Clear away any hair, string, dust, or debris wrapped around the brush and around the brush post.

4. **Reinstall the side brush.**  
   Place the brush back onto the brush post, then tighten the screw securely. Avoid over-tightening; it only needs to be firmly in place.

**Recommended cleaning frequency:** Clean the side brush **once per month**, or more often if it frequently collects hair or debris.,['Manual32_16']

The dual-mode virtual wall barrier is used to guide where the vacuum can and cannot clean. It creates an invisible boundary that the vacuum recognizes, helping keep it in the areas you want cleaned while preventing it from entering areas you want to avoid. You can choose between two modes depending on your cleaning needs.

<PIC>

**Halo Mode — protects objects or small areas**  
Set the switch to the **down** position to use Halo Mode. In this mode, the device creates an invisible circular protective zone around itself that the vacuum will not enter. This is useful for protecting items such as pet bowls, vases, or other objects you do not want the vacuum to bump into. The protective halo reaches approximately **24 inches from the center of the device**.

<PIC>

**Virtual Wall Mode — blocks doorways or openings**  
Set the switch to the **up** position to use Virtual Wall Mode. In this mode, the device creates an invisible cone-shaped barrier that only the vacuum can detect. It can block openings of up to **10 feet**, making it helpful for keeping the vacuum out of another room, away from a hallway, or out of a specific area. The barrier becomes wider as it extends farther from the device.

<PIC>

**Battery and storage note:** Under normal use, the batteries usually last about **8–10 months**. If you do not plan to use the virtual wall barrier for an extended period, switch it to the **Off** middle position to help preserve battery life.,['Manual32_4', 'Manual32_5', 'Manual32_6']

If the vacuum indicates a problem, you can troubleshoot it as follows:

1. **Check the alert from the vacuum**  
   When something is wrong, the vacuum will play a **two-tone distress sound**, then give a **spoken message** describing the issue. At the same time, the **Troubleshooting Indicator** will blink.

<PIC>

In the image, you can identify the main controls and indicators: the large **CLEAN** button is in the center, and the **Troubleshooting Indicator** is the warning-triangle icon above the buttons.

2. **Listen to the spoken message**  
   The spoken message can help identify what needs attention, such as the bin, battery, sensors, wheels, brushes, or other parts that may need checking or cleaning.

3. **Try rebooting the vacuum**  
   For some errors, a reboot may clear the problem:
   - Press and hold the **CLEAN** button for **10 seconds** until all indicators illuminate.
   - Release the **CLEAN** button.
   - You should hear a tone confirming the reboot was successful.

4. **Check the app for more details**  
   If the issue continues, open the app to view current status information, guided help, and support videos.

**Note:** If you use the scheduling feature, open the app after rebooting to confirm that your cleaning schedule is still set correctly.,['Manual32_2']

To mount the camera on a drop-ceiling T-rail, use the included T-rail mounting hardware. The hardware is designed to fit most 9/16", 15/16", or 1 1/2" T-rails.

1. Position the T-rail clips on the ceiling rail. Rotate each clip onto the T-rail and snap it into place. Make sure the clips sit firmly on the rail; the black foam pads should be slightly compressed after installation, which helps the clips grip securely.

<PIC>

2. Use the dashed guide lines on the mount plate template to set the correct spacing between the T-rail clips. Align the clips with the template markings so they match the mount plate holes marked “G,” which are used for drop-ceiling T-rail mounting.

<PIC>

3. Tighten the set screws on the T-rail clips. Use the included 5/64" / 2 mm hex key to secure the clips so they do not shift on the rail.

<PIC>

4. Attach the mount plate to the T-rail clips using the mount plate holes marked “G.” Insert the screws through the mount plate and into the center of the T-rail clips, then tighten them evenly so the plate sits flat and stable.

<PIC>

5. Select the correct screws based on the T-rail type:
   - For standard T-rail mounting, use the standard 6-32 x 4 mm T-rail screws.
   - For recessed T-rail mounting, use the rubber spacers together with the longer 6-32 x 7 mm screws. The spacer sits between the mount plate and the T-rail clip to compensate for the recessed rail depth.

<PIC>

After the mount plate is firmly secured, you can attach the camera to the mount plate and complete cable connection and final alignment.,['Manual33_10', 'Manual33_11', 'Manual33_12', 'Manual33_13', 'Manual33_14']

2. Connect one end of the Ethernet cable to the camera.
3. Connect the other end of the Ethernet cable to an active 802.3af PoE switch port or to a PoE injector.
4. Route and loop the cable through the camera base as shown below, making sure it sits securely in the cable channel and exits cleanly without being pinched.
