Note: Power over Ethernet supports a maximum Ethernet cable length of 300 ft / 100 m. After power is applied, the camera LED may flash green while it initializes or updates, and should turn solid green once operating normally.,['Manual33_3']

The brake lever is used to slow or stop the machine by braking the entire drive system. To stop, first release the throttle, then squeeze the brake lever smoothly toward the handlebar. Avoid grabbing the brake suddenly, especially on ice or hard-packed snow, because abrupt braking can cause the drive track to lose traction and reduce control.

<PIC>

When using the brake lever, make sure the brake lever end does not extend beyond the handlebar end. Keeping the lever end within the handlebar profile helps prevent damage to the brake lever if the machine is placed on its side for service.

The parking brake button is used when parking the machine or before starting the engine. To apply the parking brake:
1. Squeeze the brake lever toward the handlebar.
2. While holding the lever, push down the parking brake button.
3. Release the brake lever so the button holds the brake in the locked position.

To release the parking brake, simply squeeze the brake lever again; the parking brake button will release.

<PIC>

Important safety notes:
- Always set the parking brake before starting the engine.
- Never operate or drive the machine with the parking brake applied. Doing so can overheat the brake disc and reduce braking ability.
- When slowing down or stopping during riding, release the throttle first and apply the brake gently and progressively, not suddenly.,['Manual34_39', 'Manual34_40']

Before using a snowmobile, please complete the following preparation checks. These checks help confirm the machine is ready to operate and reduce the risk of loss of control, fire, or mechanical damage.

1. Review safety labels and know the controls
Before riding, read all safety and warning labels on the snowmobile and make sure you understand the function of the throttle, brake, steering, parking brake, engine stop switch, and other operating controls. Do not operate the machine if any control or function is unclear.
<PIC>

2. Check fuel level and refueling safety
Make sure there is enough fuel in the tank before starting out. After refueling, confirm that the fuel tank cap is tightened securely. Refuel outdoors with care, keep snow or ice out of the tank, and never refuel while smoking, near an open flame, or while the engine is running or hot. Wipe up any spilled fuel immediately.
<PIC>

3. Check engine oil level
Confirm that the oil tank has sufficient oil and that the level is within the proper range. Running with insufficient oil can cause serious engine damage, so top up with the recommended 2-cycle oil when needed.
<PIC>

4. Check throttle operation
Before starting the engine, press and release the throttle lever to make sure it moves smoothly and returns fully to the idle position when released. If the throttle sticks, feels abnormal, or does not return properly, do not operate the snowmobile until it has been inspected and corrected.
<PIC>

5. Check brake and parking brake
Verify that the brake operates properly before starting. Apply the parking brake before starting the engine, but never ride with the parking brake applied, as this can overheat the brake and reduce braking ability.

6. Check steering system
Turn the handlebars and check the skis for smooth movement and proper response. Also check for excessive looseness or play in the steering system. Do not ride if steering feels loose, binding, or abnormal.
<PIC>

7. Inspect key mechanical parts
Always perform a full pre-operation inspection, including:
- Manual starter condition
- Throttle Override System (T.O.R.S.)
- V-belt and drive V-belt guard
- Drive track condition and adjustment
- Slide runners
- Skis and ski runners
- Lights
- Battery
- Fittings and fasteners
- Service tools and spare parts

Do not operate the engine without the drive belt or drive guard installed. Also, do not operate the snowmobile if the drive track is damaged.

8. Wear proper protective clothing
Wear an approved helmet, face shield or goggles, warm snowmobile suit, boots, and snowmobile gloves or mittens that still allow proper thumb and finger movement for control operation.
<PIC>

9. Final safety reminders before riding
- Do not operate the snowmobile after drinking alcohol or taking drugs.
- Do not allow anyone to stand behind the machine while starting, inspecting, or adjusting it.
- Do not operate on public roads.
- Ride only in suitable snow or ice conditions; avoid dirt, sand, rocks, grass, and bare pavement.
- For longer trips, carry basic tools, spare parts, and extra fuel and oil as needed.,['snowmobile_01', 'Manual34_93', 'Manual34_96', 'Manual34_97', 'Manual34_108', 'Manual34_11']

Before starting the snowmobile engine, please make sure the machine is in a safe starting condition:

- Apply the parking brake before attempting to start the engine.
- Check that the throttle, brake, and steering operate properly.
- Make sure the engine stop switch is in the “ON/RUN” position.
- Do not start or run the engine in an enclosed area unless only moving the snowmobile in or out, and keep outside doors open because exhaust fumes are dangerous.
- Do not ride with the parking brake applied after the engine has started.

The engine stop switch should be pulled up to the running position as shown below:

<PIC>

Step 1: Set the starter lever/choke

Fully open the starter lever, also called the choke, especially for a cold engine start.

Starter lever positions:
1. Starter lever/choke
2. Fully open: for cold engine starting
3. Half-open: for warming up the engine, applicable to CS340E/ET410TR/VK540E
4. Close: for warm engine starting

For a cold start, begin with the choke fully open. After the engine starts, move it to the half-open position where applicable, then close it once the engine runs smoothly.

<PIC>

For a manual starting model:

1. Turn the main switch to the “ON” position.
2. Pull the recoil starter slowly until you feel it engage.
3. Once engaged, pull the starter handle briskly to start the engine.
4. After the engine starts, move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position.
5. Let the engine warm up until it runs smoothly and does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

The correct manual starting action is to first take up the starter engagement gently, then pull firmly and quickly:

<PIC>

For an electric starting model:

1. Turn the main switch to the “START” position.
2. Release the switch immediately after the engine starts.
3. After the engine starts, move the starter lever/choke to the “Half-open” position.
4. Warm up the engine until it runs smoothly and does not run roughly or begin to stall when the starter lever is returned to the “Close” position.

The main switch should be turned to the “START” position as shown below:

<PIC>

Important caution for electric starting:

- Release the switch immediately once the engine starts.
- If the engine does not start, release the switch, wait a few seconds, and then try again.
- Keep each starting attempt as short as possible to help preserve the battery.
- Do not crank the engine for more than 10 seconds during any single attempt.

After the engine has started and warmed up, remember to release the parking brake before riding. Never operate the snowmobile with the parking brake still applied.,['Manual34_111', 'Manual34_115', 'Manual34_116', 'Manual34_117']

A V-Belt Holder is the dedicated storage holder for keeping a spare V-belt on the machine for emergency use. It helps ensure you have a replacement belt available if the installed V-belt becomes worn or damaged during operation.

To store or remove the spare V-belt, loosen the bolt marked ①. This releases the holder so the belt can be taken out or positioned correctly.

<PIC>

When installing the spare V-belt into the holder, please pay attention to the following points:

1. Tighten the bolt securely after placing the V-belt in the holder. This prevents the belt from moving or coming loose while the machine is operating.

<PIC>

2. Make sure the V-belt is seated properly in the holder and aligned with the retaining strap/holder area.

<PIC>

3. While tightening the holder, check that the fuel hose is not pinched and remains correctly positioned. A pinched fuel hose may affect fuel flow or become damaged.

<PIC>

4. Confirm the V-belt is firmly secured in the holder before operating the machine. If the spare belt comes loose, it may contact the hot muffler and become damaged.

<PIC>

In short, the V-Belt Holder is a built-in place to safely carry a spare V-belt, but the belt must be secured tightly and kept clear of the fuel hose and hot muffler area.,['Manual34_51', 'Manual34_52', 'Manual34_53', 'Manual34_54', 'Manual34_55']

CAUTION: Before adjusting the throttle cable, make sure the engine idle speed has already been correctly adjusted.

Follow these steps to adjust the throttle cable on the snowmobile:

1. Locate the throttle cable adjuster assembly.
   - ② is the locknut.
   - ③ is the adjuster.
   - ① indicates throttle lever free play.

2. Loosen the adjuster locknut ②.

3. Turn the adjuster ③ in or out until the throttle lever free play ① is within the specified range for your model:

<PIC>

Throttle lever free play ①:
- BR250T, ET410TR, VK540E: 1.0–2.0 mm (0.04–0.08 in)
- CS340E: 0.5–1.0 mm (0.02–0.04 in)

Use the following diagrams to identify the free play point, locknut, and adjuster location:

<PIC>

<PIC>

4. Once the correct free play is achieved, tighten the locknut ② securely while holding the adjuster ③ in position so the setting does not change.

5. After adjustment, operate the throttle lever several times and confirm that it moves smoothly and returns properly. Do not ride the snowmobile if the throttle does not return smoothly.,['Manual34_148', 'Manual34_149', 'Manual34_150']

To use the steering system on a snowmobile properly, start with a safety check before every ride, then use both the handlebars and your body position to guide the machine smoothly.

1. Before starting the engine, check the steering system

Before riding, confirm that the throttle, brake, and steering all operate correctly. For the steering system, move the handlebars and skis to check for smooth movement and make sure there is no excessive play or looseness in the steering linkage or steering column.

<PIC>

If the handlebars or skis feel loose, bind, stick, or do not respond normally, do not ride until the issue has been inspected and corrected.

2. Basic steering while riding

When moving, turn the handlebar in the direction you want to go. Apply the throttle smoothly and avoid sudden inputs. The snowmobile responds best when steering is combined with correct body movement, especially on snow surfaces where traction can vary.

3. Use “body English” when turning

For most snow conditions, turning is not done by the handlebars alone. As you approach a curve:

- Slow down before entering the turn.
- Begin turning the handlebars in the desired direction.
- Shift your weight onto the running board on the inside of the turn.
- Lean your upper body into the turn to help the snowmobile stay balanced.

<PIC>

The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more you may need to lean into the turn. However, speed should always be kept within your skill level and the conditions.

4. Practice at low speed first

Practice turning many times at low speed in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles or traffic. Get comfortable with throttle control, braking, handlebar movement, and body positioning before attempting tighter turns, higher speeds, slopes, or rough terrain.

5. Important safety reminders

- Keep both hands on the handlebars while riding.
- Keep your feet on the running boards; do not place them outside the snowmobile.
- Avoid abrupt throttle changes, sudden braking, or incorrect body movement while turning.
- Do not enter a turn too fast for its sharpness or for the snow conditions.
- If the snowmobile begins to tip during a turn, lean farther into the turn to regain balance. If needed, gradually release the throttle and/or steer slightly toward the outside of the turn.
- Avoid high speeds until you are fully familiar with the snowmobile’s handling and controls.

In short: check the steering before riding, steer smoothly with the handlebars, slow down before turns, and use your body weight to lean into the turn for better balance and control.,['Manual34_108', 'Manual34_127']

For most snow conditions, the key to making a proper turn on a snowmobile is using your body position—often called “body English”—together with smooth steering and speed control.

1. **Slow down before the curve**  
   As you approach the turn, reduce your speed early. Do not wait until you are already in the curve to brake hard.

2. **Turn the handlebars in the direction you want to go**  
   Begin steering smoothly toward the inside of the turn. Avoid sudden handlebar movements.

3. **Shift your weight to the inside running board**  
   Put more of your weight on the running board on the inside of the turn. This helps the snowmobile stay balanced and respond more predictably.

4. **Lean your upper body into the turn**  
   At the same time, lean your upper body toward the inside of the curve. The sharper the turn or the higher the speed, the more body lean is generally needed.

<PIC>

5. **Practice at low speed first**  
   Practice this technique many times in a large, flat, open area with no obstacles. Once you are comfortable, you can gradually practice tighter curves or slightly higher speeds.

6. **Avoid actions that can cause tipping**  
   A snowmobile may tip during a turn if you use abrupt throttle changes, brake too hard, move your body incorrectly, or enter the turn too fast for its sharpness.

7. **If the snowmobile starts to tip**  
   Lean farther into the turn to help regain balance. If needed, gradually ease off the throttle and/or steer slightly toward the outside of the turn—do this smoothly, not suddenly.

**Important:** Keep speeds low until you are fully familiar with how your snowmobile handles. Smooth control, correct body position, and early speed reduction are the safest way to turn effectively.,['Manual34_127']

When riding uphill on a snowmobile, the key is to build momentum smoothly, manage throttle carefully, and use your body position to keep traction and balance.

<PIC>

1. Start with gentle slopes first  
Practice on mild hills before attempting steeper or more difficult climbs. Uphill riding requires good throttle control, balance, and quick reaction, so build your skill gradually.

2. Gain speed before the climb  
As you approach the hill, accelerate before you start climbing. Once the snowmobile begins going uphill, reduce the throttle opening slightly to help prevent the track from spinning or slipping.

3. Keep your weight on the uphill side  
Body position is very important:
- When climbing straight up a hill, lean forward to keep the skis down and maintain control.
- On steeper inclines, stand on the running boards and lean forward over the handlebars.
- Keep your weight positioned toward the uphill side at all times to help maintain balance and traction.

4. Slow down near the crest  
As you reach the top of the hill, reduce speed and stay alert. There may be obstacles, sharp drop-offs, other riders, vehicles, or people on the other side that you cannot see while climbing.

5. If you cannot continue uphill  
If the snowmobile loses momentum and cannot keep climbing, do not keep spinning the track. Instead:
- Stop the engine.
- Set the parking brake.
- Pull the rear of the snowmobile around so the machine points back downhill.
- Do not stand or move to the downhill side of the snowmobile.
- Once the snowmobile is facing downhill, restart the engine, release the parking brake, and descend carefully.

Important safety note: Side hills and steep slopes are not recommended for beginners or novice riders. Build experience gradually and avoid difficult uphill terrain until you are fully comfortable controlling the snowmobile.,['Manual34_127']

When riding downhill on a snowmobile, focus on keeping the machine controlled and preventing it from free-rolling down the slope:

1. Keep your speed as low as possible before and during the descent. Avoid entering the downhill section too fast.
2. Apply just enough throttle to keep the clutch engaged. This allows engine compression to help slow the snowmobile, giving you better control than coasting freely.
3. Use the brake often, but only with light pressure. Gentle, repeated braking is safer than squeezing the brake hard.
4. Maintain a stable riding position and stay prepared to adjust your balance as the slope changes.

<PIC>

WARNING: Be especially careful when braking while going downhill. Excessive braking can lock the track, which may cause the snowmobile to lose traction and result in loss of control.,['Manual34_128']

Crossing a slope, also called side-hilling, requires good balance and active body positioning. It is not recommended for beginners or novice snowmobile riders, especially on steep or icy slopes.

To cross a slope correctly:

1. Keep your weight on the uphill side  
As you travel across the face of the slope, lean your body toward the uphill side. This helps counter the pull of gravity and keeps the snowmobile balanced.

2. Use the recommended riding position  
A stable position is to kneel with the knee of your downhill leg on the seat, while keeping the foot of your uphill leg on the uphill running board. This position makes it easier to quickly shift your body weight if the snowmobile begins to slide or tip.

<PIC>

3. Be prepared for sideways sliding  
Snow and ice can be very slippery, so the snowmobile may begin to slide sideways on the slope. If this happens and there are no obstacles in your path, steer in the direction of the slide. Once you regain balance and control, gradually steer back toward your intended direction of travel.

4. If the snowmobile starts to tip  
If the machine begins to tip, steer downhill to help regain balance. Avoid sudden movements, as abrupt steering, braking, or throttle changes can make the snowmobile harder to control.

5. Know when to get off  
If you cannot maintain the correct balance and the snowmobile is going to tip over, dismount immediately on the uphill side. This helps reduce the risk of being caught underneath the machine.

In short, the key techniques are: lean uphill, keep your body active, use the knee-on-seat/foot-on-running-board position, steer with the slide if needed, and always get off to the uphill side if the snowmobile is about to overturn.,['Manual34_129']

Here are the recommended steps to inspect the spark plug on a snowmobile:

1. **Let the engine cool first**  
   Before inspection or repair, make sure the engine and muffler are cool. They can become extremely hot after operation, so avoid touching them while hot.

2. **Remove the spark plug periodically**  
   Remove and inspect the spark plug at regular intervals. Heat and deposits gradually cause the plug to wear, erode, or foul, which can affect starting, engine power, and overall performance.

3. **Check the spark plug color**  
   Look at the white porcelain insulator around the center electrode.  
   - A **medium to light tan color** is normal for a snowmobile being ridden under typical conditions.  
   - A **very different color**, such as an unusually white or dark/sooty appearance, may indicate an engine, intake, fuel, or carburetion issue.  
   If the color looks abnormal, do not try to diagnose the engine condition yourself; have the snowmobile checked by a qualified dealer.

4. **Confirm the correct spark plug reach/thread length**  
   The spark plug must have the proper thread length, also called reach.  
   - If the reach is **too long**, overheating and engine damage may occur.  
   - If the reach is **too short**, the plug may foul, performance may drop, and carbon can build up on exposed threads.

   Use the correct reach for your model as shown below:

   <PIC>

5. **Measure and adjust the electrode gap**  
   Before reinstalling or installing a spark plug, measure the electrode gap with a wire thickness gauge. Adjust the gap to the proper specification if needed. The illustration below shows the spark plug reach and the electrode gap locations:

   <PIC>

6. **Clean before installation**  
   Before installing the spark plug, clean the gasket seating surface and wipe any grime or deposits from the threads. This helps the plug seat correctly and prevents improper tightening.

7. **Tighten to the correct torque**  
   Install the spark plug and tighten it properly.

   **Spark plug torque:** 28 Nm / 2.8 m·kg / 20 ft·lb

8. **Use the correct plug type**  
   If you plan to change to a different type of spark plug, consult a qualified dealer first to ensure it is suitable for your snowmobile.,['Manual34_138', 'Manual34_139']

To clean a snowmobile properly, follow these steps:

1. Clean the entire snowmobile thoroughly, both inside and outside. This helps remove corrosive salts, acids, dirt, and other buildup that can damage metal parts and finishes over time.

2. Apply Mud and Grease Release, or an equivalent cleaning product, to areas with mud, grease, grime, or heavy residue. Allow it to loosen the buildup before washing.

3. Wash the snowmobile using mild soap and water. Avoid harsh cleaners that could damage painted, plastic, rubber, or metal surfaces.

4. Rinse the machine thoroughly with clean water to remove all soap and loosened dirt.

5. Dry the snowmobile completely. Pay attention to hidden areas where water may collect, as trapped moisture can contribute to corrosion.

Note: If the snowmobile was transported on roads where road salt is used, clean it as soon as you arrive at your destination. Road salt is highly corrosive, so removing it promptly helps protect the suspension, frame, and other metal components.

Here, “communication channels” refers to TV/CATV broadcast channels. If you want to add a channel so it can be selected normally, use Memory/On. If you want to remove a channel from the stored channel list, use Erase/Off.

You can memorize or erase channels in two ways:

1. Recommended method: Using the MENU

This method is best when you want to clearly confirm the channel number and whether it is stored or removed.

1. Press the MENU button, then press ▲/▼ until the CH. menu appears.
2. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to select Manual.

<PIC>

3. Press ▶ to enter the Manual settings.
4. Use ▲/▼ to select Channel, then press ▶ and use ▲/▼ to choose the channel number you want to memorize or erase.

When a channel is selected, the screen may show the current channel and its memory status, such as “CATV 95 Memory.”

<PIC>

5. Use ▲/▼ to select Memory.
6. Press ▶, then use ▲/▼ to choose:
   - On: memorize/add the selected channel

<PIC>

Poor TV or radio reception is usually caused by signal interference, signal reflection, weak signal strength, or antenna/cable issues. The most common conditions are below:

1. Ignition or electrical interference
This type of interference can make the picture flutter, drift, show black spots, or display horizontal streaks. It is often caused by nearby automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electric drills, motors, or other electrical appliances operating close to the receiver or antenna.
<PIC>

2. Ghosts or multiple images
Ghosting happens when the broadcast signal reaches the TV through more than one path. One signal arrives directly, while another is reflected by tall buildings, hills, large metal structures, or other objects. This can create a second, faint image on the screen. Repositioning or changing the direction of the antenna may help reduce this issue.
<PIC>

3. Snow or weak signal area
A snowy picture usually means the received signal is weak. This can happen if the receiver is located in a fringe or low-signal area, if the antenna is not suitable for the location, or if the antenna/cable condition is poor. In some cases, using a better-positioned or higher-quality antenna may improve reception.
<PIC>

Other possible causes include:
- Strong random interference from vehicles, aircraft, or nearby equipment.
- Weak antenna signal or poor broadcast signal quality.
- Old, damaged, or weathered antennas and antenna cables.
- Loose, incorrect, or corroded antenna connections.
- Local interference from nearby electrical appliances.
- Station-side problems, where only one channel is affected.

Recommended checks:
- Try another channel to confirm whether the issue is with one station or all reception.
- Check that the antenna or cable connection is secure and correctly connected.
- Adjust the antenna direction or position.
- Check for nearby electrical devices that may be causing interference and move them away if possible.
- If reception remains weak, consider using a more suitable outdoor antenna or replacing damaged antenna cables.
- If channels cannot be tuned properly, run Auto Program again to store available channels.

The chart below summarizes common reception-related symptoms and the checks that may help resolve them:
<PIC>,['television0_01', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_45']

Closed captioning is available on some TV models. It converts the audio portion of a program—such as dialogue and narration—into written text displayed on the screen, similar to subtitles. This is especially helpful for viewers who are hearing-impaired or for situations where you want to follow the program with lower volume.

<PIC>

You can enable or customize the Caption/Text setting in either of the following two ways:

### Option 1: Using the CAPTION button on the remote
1. Press the **CAPTION** button.
2. Press the **CAPTION** button repeatedly to cycle through the available choices: **OFF**, **Mode1**, **Mode2**, **Text1**, or **Text2**.
3. Press the **ENTER (O)** button to confirm and exit.

The on-screen display will show the current caption mode, such as **Caption: Mode1**, so you can confirm which setting is selected.

<PIC>

### Option 2: Using the MENU button
1. Press the **MENU** button.
2. Use the **up/down** buttons to select the **Special** menu.
3. Press the **right arrow** button, then use the **up/down** buttons to select **Caption**.
4. Press the **right arrow** button again, then use the **up/down** buttons to choose one of the following: **Off**, **Mode1**, **Mode2**, **Text1**, or **Text2**.
5. Press the **MENU** button repeatedly to exit.

The Caption menu lets you select the exact caption or text mode you want from the on-screen options.

<PIC>

### What each setting means
- **Off**: Turns captions/text off.
- **Mode1**: Usually displays the main closed captions for the program.
- **Mode2**: May display an alternate caption signal, such as demonstration or program-related information, depending on the broadcast.
- **Text1 / Text2**: Displays text services when available, such as program lists, weather information, stock updates, or news for hearing-impaired viewers. These services may use a larger portion of the screen.

### Helpful notes
- Not all TV programs or stations provide closed caption or text signals.
- If reception is weak or unstable, captions may not appear correctly.
- In Text mode, an empty black box may appear and disappear when the signal is poor; this is normal.
- The TV remembers the last caption mode you selected, even after the TV is turned off.,['Manual35_36', 'Manual35_37', 'Manual35_38']

For the best signal reception, we recommend using an outdoor antenna. Before connecting it, please check the antenna and cable condition carefully—severely weathered antennas, damaged cables, loose connectors, or corroded terminals can noticeably reduce picture and sound quality.

1. Connect the outdoor antenna cable to the TV

Choose the connection method that matches your antenna cable type:

If you are using 300 Ohm flat wire:
1. Attach the two ends of the 300 ohm flat wire securely to the screws on a 300 ohm to 75 ohm adapter.
2. Insert the adapter into the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.

If you are using a 75 Ohm coaxial cable:
1. Connect the 75 ohm coaxial cable directly to the TV’s 75 ohm antenna jack.
2. Make sure the cable is firmly seated so the connection does not loosen during use.

The image below shows both connection types and where the cable or adapter should be connected on the rear antenna jack of the TV.

<PIC>

2. Turn on the TV and run AUTO PROGRAM

After the antenna is connected, turn on the TV and run AUTO PROGRAM. This function searches for all active channels available in your area and stores them so you can select them with the channel buttons.

Using the AUTO PRG. button:
1. Press the AUTO PRG. button on the remote control.
2. When the Auto prog. start prompt appears, press the right button or press AUTO PRG. again to begin channel searching.

<PIC>

During the search, the TV will scan available channels and show the progress on screen. If you need to stop the scan before it finishes, press ENTER. Please note that only the channels found up to that point will be stored.

<PIC>

Tip: If the stored channels have poor signal quality, run AUTO PROGRAM again after checking the antenna connection or adjusting the antenna direction.

3. If reception is poor, adjust the antenna position

If the picture shows double images or “ghosts,” the TV signal may be reaching the antenna through more than one path, such as a direct signal plus a reflected signal from buildings, hills, or other large objects. In this case, slowly change the direction or position of the outdoor antenna and check whether the image improves.

<PIC>

If the picture has many small dots or looks snowy, the TV may be receiving a weak signal, especially if you are in a fringe or low-signal area. Repositioning the antenna higher, pointing it more accurately toward the broadcast source, or using a more suitable outdoor antenna may help improve the picture.

<PIC>

4. If the picture is still unstable, use Fine adjustment

If the channel is found but the picture is still unstable—for example, horizontal stripes, a twisted picture, or no color—use the Fine adjustment function to tune that channel more precisely.

Menu path:
MENU → CH. menu → Manual → Fine → adjust with left/right buttons → ENTER to memorize

Steps:
1. Press MENU and go to the CH. menu.
2. Select Manual.
3. Select Fine.
4. Use the left/right buttons to adjust the picture until it becomes more stable.
5. Press ENTER to save the adjustment.

<PIC>

<PIC>

Additional notes:
· If the signal quality is poor after programming, check the antenna connection and run AUTO PROGRAM again.
· If you see lines, ghosts, snowy picture, or cannot tune the desired channel, check the antenna cable, make sure the connection is secure, and adjust the antenna direction or position.
· Fine adjustment is best used after the antenna has been properly connected, the channels have been programmed, and the antenna position has already been optimized.,['Manual35_39', 'Manual35_5', 'Manual35_7', 'television0_02', 'television0_03', 'Manual35_23', 'Manual35_24']

To connect a DVD player to the TV or audio system for the best performance, use the component video connection together with the left/right audio connection.

1. Connect the component video cables
- On the DVD player, locate the component video output jacks: Y, Pb/PB, and Pr/PR.
- On the TV, locate the COMPONENT DVD INPUT 480i jacks: Y, PB, and PR.
- Connect each cable to the matching color/label:
  - Y → Y
  - Pb/PB → PB
  - Pr/PR → PR

2. Connect the audio cables
- Connect the DVD player’s audio output jacks to the TV’s AUDIO IN L/R jacks.
- Match the channels correctly:
  - L audio output → L audio input
  - R audio output → R audio input

<PIC>

Important note: Some DVD players may use different labels for the component video outputs. If the labels are not exactly Y/PB/PR, match them as follows:
- Y → Y
- Cb / B-Y / Pb / PB → PB
- Cr / R-Y / Pr / PR → PR

<PIC>

After connecting:
1. Turn on the TV.
2. Turn on the DVD player.
3. Press the TV/VIDEO button on the remote control or on the TV front panel.
4. Select COMPONENT as the input source.
5. Start playback on the DVD player.

Tip: The TV remembers the last selected input mode even after it is turned off, so if COMPONENT was used last, it may already be selected the next time you power on the TV.,['Manual35_43', 'Manual35_44']

I understand how frustrating it can be when a product does not function as expected, and I am here to help you resolve these technical issues as quickly as possible. To troubleshoot effectively, you should first verify the power source and connection stability to ensure the device is receiving adequate power and is properly connected to any necessary peripherals. Next, please check for any available software or firmware updates, as many malfunctions are caused by outdated versions that can be easily fixed with a patch. Performing a factory reset is another highly effective step for resolving software-related glitches, though you should ensure any important data is backed up beforehand. Additionally, please inspect the hardware for any visible physical damage or loose components that might be affecting performance. If the issue persists after these checks, please provide your order details and a description of the problem so we can offer further technical assistance or arrange for a replacement or repair immediately.

To ensure your safety and the proper functioning of the equipment, please strictly follow the instructions provided in the user manual before starting the operation. Make sure to wear appropriate protective gear such as gloves or safety glasses if required and ensure that the workspace is dry, well-ventilated, and free from obstructions. Always double-check that the power source is compatible with the product specifications and never leave the device unattended while it is in use. If you encounter any unusual noise, smell, or malfunction, please disconnect the power immediately and contact our technical support team. To provide you with more specific safety guidelines, please let me know which product you are referring to so I can assist you better.

You can adjust the intensity of your electric toothbrush to match your comfort level and oral care needs. The toothbrush offers 3 intensity settings, shown by the indicator lights on the handle:

- High intensity: three lights — for a stronger clean
- Medium intensity: two lights — for everyday balanced cleaning
- Low intensity: one light — for a gentler brushing experience, such as when your gums feel sensitive

<PIC>

To manually change the intensity, press the intensity indicator area on the handle. Each press cycles through the available settings, so you can stop when the number of lights matches your preferred intensity.

You can change the intensity at any time: before brushing, while brushing, or after brushing.

<PIC>

You can also customize the intensity setting through the app if your toothbrush is connected.

Please note: the toothbrush includes Adaptive Intensity to help protect your gums. If you brush with too much pressure for an extended period, the toothbrush will automatically reduce the intensity by 1 level. At the start of your next brushing session, it will return to your pre-selected intensity setting.,['toothbrush0_06', 'toothbrush0_07']

You can customize your toothbrush by activating or deactivating the following features: - Adaptive Intensity - Pressure Sensor Feedback - Scrubbing Feedback - Brush head replacement reminder Please note: If Pressure Sensor Feedback is deactivated, Adaptive Intensity will also be deactivated. There are two ways to adjust these features: through the app or directly from the toothbrush handle. 1. Activate or deactivate features from the app From the app, you can turn the following features on or off: - Adaptive Intensity - Scrubbing Feedback To use the app controls: 1. Download and open the app on your phone. 2. Make sure Bluetooth is turned on. 3. Pick up the toothbrush so the handle becomes active and the lights turn on. 4. Follow the guided steps in the app. 5. Pair your toothbrush with the app. 6. Once connected, go to the toothbrush settings to customize the available features. 2. Activate or deactivate features from the handle You can also control several features directly from the toothbrush handle. Step 1: Place the toothbrush handle on the charging stand. Step 2: Press and hold the power button. The feature selected depends on how long you hold the button: - Adaptive Intensity: Hold for up to 3 seconds. You will hear 1 beep, and the SenseIQ indicator will light up for 3 seconds. - Pressure Sensor Feedback: Hold for up to 5 seconds. You will hear 1 beep followed by 2 beeps, and the SenseIQ indicator plus the light ring will light up purple for 3 seconds. - Brush head replacement reminder: Hold for up to 7 seconds. You will hear 1 beep, then 2 beeps, then 3 beeps, and the brush head replacement reminder indicator will light up for 3 seconds. After selecting the feature, check the battery indicator and tones to confirm whether the feature was activated or deactivated: - If the battery indicator blinks white 3 times and you hear 3 tones from low to high, the feature has been activated. - If the battery indicator blinks amber 3 times and you hear 3 tones from high to low, the feature has been deactivated. 3. Adjust brushing intensity for daily use For regular brushing, you can also customize the intensity level directly on the handle. Press the intensity indicator area to cycle through the three settings: - High intensity: three lights - Medium intensity: two lights - Low intensity: one light You can change the intensity before brushing, during brushing, or after brushing. ,['Manual37_13', 'toothbrush0_11', 'toothbrush0_07']

To charge your electric toothbrush while it is inside the travel case, please follow these steps:

1. Connect the USB-C cord to the charging port on the travel case, then connect the other end to the USB wall adapter.

<PIC>

2. Plug the USB wall adapter into an electrical outlet.

3. Place the toothbrush handle into the travel case, making sure it sits correctly in its charging position. If you are storing the brush head as well, place it in the designated slot.

<PIC>

4. Check that charging has started properly. When the toothbrush is correctly positioned and charging begins, you should notice the following:
- The toothbrush beeps twice.
- The lights illuminate in an upward motion.
- The battery indicator blinks white while charging.

<PIC>

5. Leave the travel case plugged in until the toothbrush is fully charged. When charging is complete, the battery light will stop blinking and turn off.

<PIC>

For best results, place the travel case on its side while charging to keep it stable. Also, use only the compatible USB wall adapter, charging base, and charging travel case for charging. Charge the toothbrush in an environment between 0 °C and 40 °C, and do not use any charging accessory if it is damaged.,['Manual37_18', 'Manual37_19', 'Manual37_20', 'Manual37_21']
